## Context and Problem Statement

How do we want to organise work in branches and how should changes be released? How should different branches be continuously deployed for QA?

## Decision Drivers

* We need to have confidence in our releases.
* We want more structured releases while we're still getting our footing in a shared monorepo.
* We need simplicity and clear [takt time] so different teams can plan for what is going out the door from them.
* It should work well with our agile work environment.
human_decision:
# Branching and release strategy

* Status: accepted
* Deciders: devs, devops
* Date: 2020-05-24





## Considered Options

Branching strategies:

* [Git Flow]
* [GitHub Flow]
* [OneFlow]

Release strategies:

* [Continuous delivery]
* [Release trains]

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: "OneFlow" because it provides a single eternal branch with well structured releases.

We'll implement OneFlow with these details:

* Release branches are set up the Monday after each sprint. This is sometimes called release trains, where features line up for different release trains.
* Release and quality managers from each team are responsible for reviewing and approving releases.
* Releases apply to all apps in the monorepo.
* Releases are versioned like this: `{cycle}.{sprint}.{hotfix}`. So version 3.1.2 is the release after cycle 3, sprint 1 with two hot fixes applied.
* Feature branches are merged using "Squash and merge", so they can be easily reverted.
* There are two ways to build larger features.
  * If the feature is isolated and not likely to cause conflicts, they can stay on long-living feature branches until they are ready to be released.
  * If the feature touches many parts of the codebase, it can be useful to merge changes more often but hide the feature in production with feature flags.
* If a project needs to deploy updates outside of the sprint rhythm, they should use hotfix branches.

### Future strategy

With time, we expect to build up better testing capabilities which gives us more confidence in the health of our monorepo. Then we can move quicker, with a simpler GitHub Flow branching strategy and continuous delivery into production.

### Hosting environments

We'll set up continuous delivery to different hosting environments:

Environment | Git source            | Databases/services | Features
------------|-----------------------|--------------------|----------
sandbox     | feature branch        | Test               | All
dev         | master                | Test               | All
staging     | master                | Prod               | All
pre-prod    | release/hotfix branch | Prod               | Finished
prod        | latest release tag    | Prod               | Finished

We'll probably start with dev, staging, pre-prod and prod environments, since feature branch deployments are more dynamic and difficult to manage.

## Pros and Cons of Branching Options

### Git Flow

* Good, when there needs to be multiple versions in production.
* Good, because it enforces an easy to comprehend naming convention for branches.
* Good, because it has good support in popular git tools.
* Bad, because the git history becomes unreadable.
* Bad, because the master/develop split is redundant.

### GitHub Flow

* Good, because it fits well with Continuous Delivery and Continuous Integration.
* Good, a simpler alternative to Git Flow.
* Good, when we need to maintain a single version in production.
* Bad, because production code can become unstable most easily.
* Bad, if we need release plans.

### One Flow

* Good, because it has a clean git history (with squash-and-merge).
* Good, because it has a structured release process without multiple long running branches.
* Good, because it has good parts from GitHub Flow (simple history with one branch + feature branches) and Git Flow (releases and hot fixes).
* Bad, because it makes continuous integration more difficult.

## Pros and Cons of Release Options

### Continous Delivery

* Good, because it is simpler and leaner, allowing changes to be deployed quicker.
* Bad, because it can easily break something in production if our CI isn't good enough.

### Release trains

* Good, because it has more structured releases, which helps with planning and QA.
* Good, because it can be synchronised to organisational schedule.
* Good, because it gives us a process to verify and test each release.
* Bad, because it is fairly rigid and complicated to implement organisation-wide.

## Links

* [4 branching workflows for Git](https://medium.com/@patrickporto/4-branching-workflows-for-git-30d0aaee7bf)
* [Branching patterns - Martin Fowler](https://martinfowler.com/articles/branching-patterns.html)

[Git Flow]: https://nvie.com/posts/a-successful-git-branching-model/
[GitHub Flow]: https://guides.github.com/introduction/flow/
[OneFlow]: https://www.endoflineblog.com/oneflow-a-git-branching-model-and-workflow
[Continuous delivery]: https://martinfowler.com/bliki/ContinuousDelivery.html
[Release trains]: https://martinfowler.com/articles/branching-patterns.html#release-train
[takt time]: https://kanbanize.com/continuous-flow/takt-time
debate_answer: The Git Flow Model is the preferred solution for organizing work in branches and releasing changes, as it provides the necessary structure and confidence in releases while supporting parallel development and clear takt time.
reason: The negative side provides a compelling argument for the Git Flow Model, emphasizing the need for structured processes and confidence in releases, which are crucial for an organization still getting its footing in a shared monorepo. The Git Flow Model's ability to support parallel development and provide clear takt time aligns well with the decision drivers, despite its complexity. The negative side also addresses the cons effectively, suggesting mitigation strategies for complexity and slower releases.
message_history:
To address the problem of organizing work in branches and releasing changes, while ensuring continuous deployment for QA, we can consider the following solutions:

### Solution 1: Git Flow Model

**Description:**
The Git Flow model involves using multiple branches for different stages of development. Typically, it includes branches like `master` (or `main`), `develop`, `feature`, `release`, and `hotfix`.

**Pros:**
- **Structured Process:** Clearly defined branches for different stages of development provide structure.
- **Parallel Development:** Allows multiple teams to work on features simultaneously without interfering with each other.
- **Release Confidence:** The `release` branch allows for thorough testing before merging into `master`.

**Cons:**
- **Complexity:** Managing multiple branches can become complex and may require more coordination.
- **Overhead:** Frequent merging and branch management can introduce overhead.
- **Slower Releases:** The process can slow down the release cycle due to the need for multiple merges and testing phases.

### Solution 2: Trunk-Based Development

**Description:**
Trunk-based development involves a single main branch (trunk) where all developers integrate their changes. Feature flags are used to control the release of new features.

**Pros:**
- **Simplicity:** Fewer branches to manage, reducing complexity.
- **Fast Releases:** Changes can be released quickly as they are integrated continuously.
- **Agile Alignment:** Supports agile practices by allowing for rapid iteration and feedback.

**Cons:**
- **Risk of Instability:** Direct integration into the main branch can lead to instability if not managed properly.
- **Feature Flags Management:** Requires robust feature flagging to ensure incomplete features do not affect production.
- **Requires Discipline:** Developers need to be disciplined in writing tests and ensuring code quality.

### Solution 3: Release Train Model

**Description:**
The release train model involves scheduled releases at regular intervals, regardless of whether all features are ready. Features not ready are deferred to the next release.

**Pros:**
- **Predictability:** Regular release schedules provide predictability for planning.
- **Reduced Pressure:** Teams are not pressured to rush features, as they can be deferred.
- **Confidence in Releases:** Regular testing and integration build confidence in the release quality.

**Cons:**
- **Inflexibility:** Features that miss the release train have to wait for the next cycle.
- **Potential Waste:** Resources may be spent on features that are not ready for release.
- **Coordination Required:** Requires coordination to ensure features are ready for the scheduled release.

### Best Solution Selection

Considering the decision drivers, the **Trunk-Based Development** approach seems to be the best fit. It aligns well with an agile work environment by allowing rapid iteration and feedback. Although it requires discipline and robust feature flagging, it offers simplicity and fast releases, which are crucial for maintaining clear takt time and structured releases in a shared monorepo. The potential risk of instability can be mitigated with automated testing and continuous integration practices.
While I understand the appeal of Trunk-Based Development due to its simplicity and alignment with agile practices, I would argue that the **Git Flow Model** might be a more suitable choice given the context and decision drivers provided.

### Reasons for Choosing Git Flow Model

1. **Confidence in Releases:**
   - The Git Flow Model inherently provides a structured process with multiple stages of testing and validation. The presence of a `release` branch allows for thorough testing and bug fixing before changes are merged into the `master` branch. This can significantly boost confidence in the stability and quality of releases.

2. **Structured Releases:**
   - As the organization is still getting its footing in a shared monorepo, having a structured branching strategy like Git Flow can help teams understand the flow of changes and the release process. This structure can be particularly beneficial in a complex environment where multiple teams are working on different features simultaneously.

3. **Parallel Development:**
   - The Git Flow Model supports parallel development through feature branches, allowing teams to work independently without affecting each other's work. This can be crucial in a shared monorepo where multiple teams need to collaborate and coordinate their efforts.

4. **Clear Takt Time:**
   - By having dedicated branches for different stages of development, teams can better plan and predict when their changes will be released. This clarity in takt time can help in aligning with business goals and ensuring that teams are on the same page regarding release schedules.

5. **Agile Compatibility:**
   - While Trunk-Based Development is often seen as more agile, the Git Flow Model can also be adapted to agile practices. By using short-lived feature branches and frequent integration into the `develop` branch, teams can still achieve rapid iteration and feedback.

### Addressing the Cons

- **Complexity and Overhead:**
  - While the Git Flow Model can be complex, this can be mitigated with proper tooling and automation. Continuous integration and automated testing can help manage the overhead of merging and testing.

- **Slower Releases:**
  - The perceived slower release cycle can be addressed by optimizing the release process and ensuring that the `release` branch is used effectively for final testing and bug fixing.

### Conclusion

Given the need for confidence in releases, structured processes, and clear takt time, the Git Flow Model provides a robust framework that can support these requirements. While it may introduce some complexity, the benefits of structured releases and parallel development outweigh the potential downsides, especially in a shared monorepo environment.

## Context and Problem Statement

tracking-consent-frontend provides a solution for tax users to express their cookie consent preferences via a 
banner and cookie settings page, storing these preferences in a `userConsent` cookie with 
the following structure:

```
{
  version: string, // Currently 2020.1
  datetimeSet: string, // e.g. 2021-03-04T15:16:47.077Z
  preferences: {
    acceptAll?: boolean, e.g. true
    measurement?: boolean,
    settings?: boolean
  }
};
```

The `acceptAll` category was added to allow for future differentiation between users that chose to accept all
via the banner vs users that accepted all by individually selecting 'Use' for each cookie type on the settings page.

We now believe this feature may lead to services assuming the user has consented to the use of types of cookie that
were not actually listed at the point the user consented. For example, if marketing cookies are added in 2022,
is it possible services may assume `acceptAll: true` set in 2021 means that the user has consented to the use of marketing cookies?
Services would need to rely on the cookie version or datetimeSet in order to determine what cookie types
existed at the point the user consented.

Given the need to differentiate between users accepting all cookies via the banner vs the cookie settings is not currently a requirement
should we therefore, remove the acceptAll category and instead set individual categories, so that it is explicit in the userConsent
which categories the user has consented to?

Such a change would result in a new version of the userConsent cookie with the following structure:

```
{
  version: string, // 2021.1
  datetimeSet: string, // e.g. 2021-03-04T15:16:47.077Z
  preferences: {
    measurement?: boolean,
    settings?: boolean
  }
};
```

## Decision Drivers

* the preference for keeping the consenting data model simple, unambiguous and free from duplication
* the need to mitigate the risk of services misinterpreting the preferences stored in the userConsent cookie
* the lack of a current or anticipated future need to differentiate how a user has consented
* that storing preferences individually is consistent with the functionality implemented on GOV.UK (see links below)
human_decision:
# Always store consents individually

* Status: accepted
* Date: 2021-03-05

Technical Story: PLATUI-1049





## Considered Options

* Remove 'acceptAll' from userConsent
* Do nothing

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: "Remove 'acceptAll'", because for the reasons above we believe it is the right thing to do and makes sense
 to do before the solution goes live.

### Positive Consequences

* There is less risk user cookie preferences will be misinterpreted by services
* Teams are likely to be less confused and need to ask fewer questions around the meaning of the categories

### Negative Consequences

* The need to increment the userConsent version number and introduce logic for dealing
with old cookie versions.

## Links

* GOV.UK design system [component](https://design-system.service.gov.uk/components/cookie-banner/)
* GOV.UK publishing [documentation](https://docs.publishing.service.gov.uk/manual/cookie-consent-on-govuk.html)
* GOV.UK publishing [component](https://components.publishing.service.gov.uk/component-guide/cookie_banner)
debate_answer: Solution 3: Hybrid Approach with User Notification is the preferred solution as it balances clarity, adaptability, and user engagement, addressing both current and future challenges in cookie consent management.
reason: The negative side presents a more comprehensive solution that not only addresses the current issue of misinterpretation but also prepares for future changes in cookie types. Solution 3, the Hybrid Approach with User Notification, ensures user awareness and engagement, which aligns with modern privacy standards and regulatory compliance. It provides flexibility and adaptability, ensuring that user consent is always informed and explicit, which is crucial for maintaining trust and compliance with privacy regulations like GDPR.
message_history:
To address the problem of potential misinterpretation of the `acceptAll` category in the `userConsent` cookie, we can consider several solutions. Here are three potential solutions, along with their pros and cons:

### Solution 1: Remove the `acceptAll` Category and Use Individual Categories

**Description:**
Remove the `acceptAll` category from the `userConsent` cookie and store preferences for each cookie type individually. This would mean that each type of cookie (e.g., measurement, settings, marketing) would have its own explicit consent status.

**Pros:**
- **Clarity:** Each cookie type's consent status is explicitly stated, reducing ambiguity.
- **Simplicity:** Aligns with the decision driver to keep the data model simple and unambiguous.
- **Consistency:** Matches the approach used by GOV.UK, which may be familiar to users and developers.
- **Future-Proofing:** Avoids assumptions about future cookie types, as each new type would require explicit consent.

**Cons:**
- **Initial Effort:** Requires updating the system to handle individual categories, which may involve development time and resources.
- **User Experience:** Users may need to make more decisions if there are many cookie types, potentially leading to consent fatigue.

### Solution 2: Retain `acceptAll` but Add Versioning and Contextual Information

**Description:**
Keep the `acceptAll` category but enhance the system to include more detailed versioning and contextual information about what `acceptAll` included at the time of consent.

**Pros:**
- **Backward Compatibility:** Maintains the existing structure for users who have already consented.
- **Contextual Clarity:** Provides services with the necessary context to interpret `acceptAll` correctly based on the version and datetimeSet.

**Cons:**
- **Complexity:** Increases the complexity of the data model, as services need to interpret additional contextual information.
- **Risk of Misinterpretation:** Still relies on services to correctly interpret the versioning and context, which may not always happen.

### Solution 3: Hybrid Approach with User Notification

**Description:**
Combine the removal of `acceptAll` with a notification system that informs users when new cookie types are added, prompting them to update their preferences.

**Pros:**
- **User Awareness:** Keeps users informed about changes and ensures they have the opportunity to consent to new cookie types.
- **Explicit Consent:** Ensures that consent is explicit for each cookie type, reducing the risk of misinterpretation.

**Cons:**
- **User Burden:** Requires users to engage with the system more frequently, which may be seen as intrusive or annoying.
- **Implementation Complexity:** Requires additional infrastructure to manage notifications and user interactions.

### Best Solution

Considering the decision drivers and the need to keep the data model simple and unambiguous, **Solution 1: Remove the `acceptAll` Category and Use Individual Categories** appears to be the best option. It aligns with the preference for simplicity, reduces the risk of misinterpretation, and is consistent with existing practices on GOV.UK. While it requires some initial effort to implement, the long-term benefits of clarity and reduced ambiguity make it a strong choice.
While Solution 1 offers a straightforward approach by removing the `acceptAll` category and using individual categories, I believe Solution 3, the Hybrid Approach with User Notification, might be a more comprehensive solution. Here's why:

### Solution 3: Hybrid Approach with User Notification

**Description:**
This solution involves removing the `acceptAll` category and implementing a notification system that informs users when new cookie types are added, prompting them to update their preferences.

**Pros:**
- **User Awareness and Engagement:** By notifying users of changes, this approach ensures that users are always aware of what they are consenting to. This transparency can build trust and ensure that consent is always informed and explicit.
- **Adaptability:** As new cookie types are introduced, users are prompted to review and consent to these changes, ensuring that their preferences are always up-to-date.
- **Explicit Consent:** This approach ensures that consent is explicit for each cookie type, reducing the risk of misinterpretation and aligning with privacy regulations that emphasize informed consent.

**Cons:**
- **User Burden:** Frequent notifications might be seen as intrusive, potentially leading to user annoyance or disengagement. However, this can be mitigated by designing a user-friendly notification system that minimizes disruption.
- **Implementation Complexity:** Developing a notification system requires additional resources and infrastructure, which could be a significant initial investment.

### Why Solution 3 is Preferable

1. **User-Centric Approach:** Solution 3 places the user at the center of the consent process, ensuring they are always informed and in control of their preferences. This aligns with modern privacy standards and user expectations.

2. **Future-Proofing with Flexibility:** While Solution 1 is simple, it doesn't account for the dynamic nature of cookie types and user preferences. Solution 3, however, provides a mechanism to adapt to changes without assuming user consent for new categories.

3. **Regulatory Compliance:** With increasing emphasis on data privacy and protection, having a system that actively engages users in consent decisions can help ensure compliance with regulations like GDPR, which require clear and informed consent.

To provide a comprehensive response, I need more information about the specific context and problem statement you are referring to. However, I can offer a generic framework for approaching problem-solving, which you can adapt to your specific situation.

## Context and Problem Statement

Let's assume a hypothetical scenario: A company is experiencing a decline in employee productivity, which is affecting overall business performance. The problem statement could be: "How can the company improve employee productivity to enhance business performance?"

## Decision Drivers

* Employee satisfaction and well-being
* Cost-effectiveness of the solution
* Implementation time and complexity
* Long-term sustainability of the solution

## Proposed Solutions

### Solution 1: Implement Flexible Work Hours

**Pros:**
- Increases employee satisfaction by allowing a better work-life balance.
- Can lead to higher productivity as employees work during their most productive hours.
- May reduce absenteeism and turnover rates.

**Cons:**
- Difficult to coordinate team meetings and collaboration.
- May require changes in management and monitoring practices.
- Not suitable for all types of jobs or industries.

### Solution 2: Invest in Employee Training and Development

**Pros:**
- Enhances employee skills and competencies, leading to improved performance.
- Demonstrates company commitment to employee growth, boosting morale.
- Can lead to innovation and improved problem-solving capabilities.

**Cons:**
- Requires significant investment of time and resources.
- Immediate productivity gains may not be visible.
- Risk of trained employees leaving for better opportunities.

### Solution 3: Introduce Performance-Based Incentives

**Pros:**
- Directly links productivity to rewards, motivating employees to perform better.
- Can be tailored to individual or team performance, fostering healthy competition.
- Relatively easy to implement with clear metrics.

**Cons:**
- May lead to unhealthy competition and stress among employees.
- Can result in short-term focus, neglecting long-term goals.
- Requires careful design to ensure fairness and transparency.

### Solution 4: Improve Workplace Environment

**Pros:**
- Enhances employee comfort and satisfaction, leading to better productivity.
- Can include ergonomic furniture, better lighting, and recreational areas.
- Often results in a positive company culture and reduced stress levels.

**Cons:**
- Initial costs can be high, depending on the extent of changes.
- May not directly address specific productivity issues.
- Benefits can be subjective and vary among employees.

## Selecting the Best Solution

Considering the decision drivers, the best solution might be a combination of the above options. However, if we must choose one, investing in employee training and development could be the most sustainable and impactful solution. It addresses the root cause of productivity issues by enhancing skills and competencies, aligns with long-term business goals, and improves employee satisfaction. While it requires an upfront investment, the long-term benefits in terms of innovation, retention, and performance can outweigh the initial costs.
Certainly, I can provide an alternative perspective on the best solution for improving employee productivity.

## Alternative Solution: Implement Flexible Work Hours

While investing in employee training and development is a strong contender, I argue that implementing flexible work hours could be the most effective solution given the current context and decision drivers.

### Reasons for Choosing Flexible Work Hours

1. **Employee Satisfaction and Well-being:**
   - Flexible work hours directly address employee satisfaction by allowing individuals to tailor their work schedules to fit personal needs and peak productivity times. This can lead to a more engaged and motivated workforce, which is crucial for improving productivity.

2. **Cost-effectiveness:**
   - Unlike training programs, which require significant financial investment, flexible work hours can be implemented with minimal cost. The primary expense may involve updating policies and potentially investing in remote work technology, which many companies already have in place.

3. **Implementation Time and Complexity:**
   - Flexible work hours can be rolled out relatively quickly compared to training programs, which require planning, resources, and time to see results. Adjusting work schedules can be done with immediate effect, allowing for a quicker response to productivity issues.

4. **Long-term Sustainability:**
   - As work-life balance becomes increasingly important, flexible work arrangements are likely to remain a desirable feature for employees. This solution aligns with long-term trends in workplace flexibility and can help attract and retain talent.

### Addressing the Cons

- **Coordination Challenges:**
  - While coordinating meetings can be challenging, technology solutions like shared calendars and communication platforms can mitigate these issues. Establishing core hours where all employees are available can also help.

- **Management and Monitoring:**
  - Implementing clear guidelines and using productivity tracking tools can help managers oversee work without micromanaging. Training managers to lead remote teams effectively can also address this concern.

- **Industry Suitability:**
  - While not all jobs can accommodate flexible hours, many roles, especially in knowledge-based industries, can benefit significantly. For roles that require fixed hours, partial flexibility or hybrid models can be explored.

In conclusion, while training and development are essential for long-term growth, flexible work hours offer a more immediate, cost-effective, and employee-centric solution to the problem of declining productivity. By prioritizing employee well-being and adapting to modern work trends, companies can create a more dynamic and productive work environment.

# Modular Architecture (breaking down repositories and packages)

* Status: accepted
* Deciders: Pietro, Eimi, Dan (Data solutions)
* Date: 2019-05-10

Technical Story: Exploring a better way to organise the project than in a monorepo.

 



## Considered Options

There are 3 logically separated packages:

1. UI
2. API (monorepo)
3. (AWS/CEP/Electron) Infrastructure

* Option 1 - bundle everything (UI, API, Infra) into an RPM.
* Option 2 - separate the UI to a new github repository, bundle it as an NPM package. Keep infrastructure in the same repository as API. API will pull in UI as a dependency.
* Option 3 - separate the UI, API and infrastructure. Bundle UI and API separately as NPM packages  Infrastructure will have UI and API packages as dependencies.


## Decision Outcome

We selected Option 3.
There is a separation to all the packages, which means it will be simpler to version, test and contribute to individual packages. The API repository will contain different infrastructural flavours of the business logic. There will be a browser (Cloud) package, CEP package, and Electron package that does the same thing. Each is compatible with the UI. To package these similar packages, we can look at `lerna`. The browser flavoured package will have another Express server in the repository to simplify local testing. Infrastructure, which will have UI and API as dependencies will not have an Express server inside.

A potential problem here is that during development, the packages will have features not implemented in the other packages. In order to have parallel set of features, we will add BDD tests to automatically test that all the packages have the same features which are functional.

We are also keeping the AWS Infrastructure public, as the generic cloudformation JSON has no confidential or security issues in it.


### Deployment flow

```
Infrastructure Repo
+------------------------------+
|                              |            +-----------+         +---------+           +------------+
| * Dependency to UI and API   |            |           |         |         |           |            |
| * AWS/Cosmos specific        +----------->+  Jenkins  +-------->+   RPM   +---------->+   Cosmos   |
|   configuration files        |            |           |         |         |           |            |

+------------------------------+                                     |   |

                                                                     |   |

                                          |           +---------->+         |
    API Repo                              +-----+-----+           +---------+
    +--------------------+                      ^
    |                    |                      |
    | * Express Server   +----------------------+
    |                    |
    +--------------------+
http://asciiflow.com/


```

### Once deployed
There will be *two* express servers in an EC2 instance.
```
              EC2
              +-----------------------------------+
              | +------------+     +------------+ |
              | | Express    |     | Express    | |
              | |  +------+  |     |            | |

```
### Repo naming conventions
Prefix can be `digital-paper-edit` or `rough-cut-pro` pending name changes

- client: `-client`
- server: `-api`
- electron: `-electron`
- cep: `-cep`
- infrastructure: `-infrastructure` or `-aws`

message_history:
To address the problem of making the open-source project more digestible by separating it into distinct components, we can consider several solutions. Each solution will be evaluated based on the decision drivers: removing BBC-specific code, separating infrastructure, and future integration with Electron and CEP.

### Solution 1: Modularization of the Codebase

**Description:**  
Break down the project into smaller, independent modules or libraries. Each module would handle a specific functionality and could be developed, maintained, and deployed independently.

**Pros:**
- **Scalability:** Easier to manage and scale as each module can be updated or replaced independently.
- **Reusability:** Modules can be reused across different projects, promoting code reuse.
- **Community Contribution:** Easier for contributors to understand and contribute to specific parts of the project.
- **Future Integration:** Facilitates integration with Electron and CEP by isolating relevant modules.

**Cons:**
- **Initial Overhead:** Requires significant initial effort to refactor and modularize the existing codebase.
- **Dependency Management:** Increased complexity in managing dependencies between modules.
- **Consistency:** Ensuring consistency across modules can be challenging.

### Solution 2: Create a Core Framework with Plugins

**Description:**  
Develop a core framework that provides the essential functionalities, and allow additional features to be added as plugins. This approach can help in removing BBC-specific code by making it a plugin.

**Pros:**
- **Flexibility:** Users can choose which plugins to include, making the project more customizable.
- **Isolation:** BBC-specific code can be isolated as a plugin, making it easier to remove or replace.
- **Community Engagement:** Encourages community to develop and share plugins.

**Cons:**
- **Complexity:** Designing a robust plugin system can be complex.
- **Performance:** Potential performance overhead due to plugin architecture.
- **Maintenance:** Requires ongoing maintenance to ensure compatibility between core and plugins.

### Solution 3: Fork the Project for Open Source

**Description:**  
Create a separate fork of the project specifically for open-source purposes, removing all BBC-specific code and infrastructure dependencies.

**Pros:**
- **Clarity:** Clear separation between the BBC-specific and open-source versions.
- **Focus:** Allows the open-source version to focus solely on community needs and contributions.
- **Simplification:** Simplifies the removal of BBC-specific code.

**Cons:**
- **Duplication of Effort:** Maintaining two separate codebases can lead to duplicated effort.
- **Resource Intensive:** Requires additional resources to manage and update both versions.
- **Integration Challenges:** Future integration with Electron and CEP might need to be handled separately for each version.

### Best Solution

Considering the decision drivers and the pros and cons of each solution, **Solution 1: Modularization of the Codebase** appears to be the best option. It provides a scalable and flexible approach that aligns well with the open-source nature of the project. By breaking down the project into modules, it becomes easier to remove BBC-specific code, manage infrastructure separately, and prepare for future integration with Electron and CEP. While it requires an initial investment in refactoring, the long-term benefits of modularization, such as improved maintainability and community engagement, make it a worthwhile approach.
While Solution 1: Modularization of the Codebase is a strong contender, I would argue that **Solution 2: Create a Core Framework with Plugins** might be a more effective approach given the context and decision drivers.

### Reasons for Choosing Solution 2: Create a Core Framework with Plugins

1. **Flexibility and Customization:**
   - A core framework with plugins allows for greater flexibility and customization. Users can tailor the project to their specific needs by selecting only the plugins they require. This is particularly beneficial in an open-source environment where diverse use cases are common.

2. **Isolation of BBC-Specific Code:**
   - By isolating BBC-specific code as a plugin, it becomes straightforward to remove or replace it without affecting the core functionality. This aligns directly with the decision driver of removing BBC-specific code.

3. **Encouraging Community Engagement:**
   - A plugin-based architecture encourages community members to develop and share their own plugins, fostering a more vibrant and active community. This can lead to a richer ecosystem of features and tools around the core framework.

4. **Simplified Maintenance:**
   - While designing a plugin system can be complex, once established, it simplifies maintenance by allowing updates and changes to be made to individual plugins without impacting the entire system. This can reduce the burden on maintainers and improve the project's overall stability.

5. **Future Integration with Electron and CEP:**
   - A plugin architecture can facilitate future integration with Electron and CEP by allowing specific plugins to handle these integrations. This modular approach can make it easier to adapt to new technologies and requirements as they arise.

### Addressing the Cons

- **Complexity:** While the initial design of a plugin system can be complex, the long-term benefits of flexibility and ease of maintenance can outweigh this initial challenge.
- **Performance:** Careful design and optimization of the plugin system can mitigate potential performance overheads.
- **Maintenance:** Establishing clear guidelines and standards for plugin development can help ensure compatibility and reduce maintenance challenges.

### Conclusion

While modularization offers scalability and reusability, the plugin-based approach provides a more flexible and community-driven solution that aligns well with the open-source ethos. It allows for easier removal of BBC-specific code, encourages community contributions, and prepares the project for future integrations. Therefore, Solution 2: Create a Core Framework with Plugins is a compelling choice for making the open-source project more digestible and adaptable.

possible**.

message_history:
To address the problem statement, we need to consider programming languages that are both engaging for developers and practical for the task at hand. Here are some potential solutions:

### Solution 1: Python

**Pros:**
- **Popularity and Adoption:** Python is widely used and likely already familiar to many developers at Xebians, making it easier to adopt.
- **Library Support:** Python has extensive libraries for handling message brokers (e.g., RabbitMQ, Kafka) and web frameworks (e.g., Flask, Django) for building backends.
- **Ease of Learning:** Python is known for its readability and simplicity, which can make it fun and engaging for developers.
- **Community Support:** A large community means plenty of resources, tutorials, and third-party tools.

**Cons:**
- **Performance:** Python may not be the fastest language, which could be a concern for high-performance requirements.
- **Concurrency:** While Python has options for concurrency, such as asyncio, it may not be as robust as other languages for highly concurrent systems.

### Solution 2: Node.js (JavaScript)

**Pros:**
- **Event-Driven Architecture:** Node.js is well-suited for handling asynchronous operations, which is ideal for message-driven systems.
- **Popularity:** JavaScript is a language many developers are familiar with, especially those with front-end experience.
- **Library Support:** There are numerous libraries and frameworks (e.g., Express.js) for building backends and handling message brokers.
- **Vibrant Ecosystem:** A large number of packages available through npm.

**Cons:**
- **Callback Hell:** Can lead to complex code if not managed properly, though modern JavaScript (ES6+) has improved this with async/await.
- **Single-Threaded:** While Node.js is non-blocking, it is single-threaded, which might not be ideal for CPU-intensive tasks.

### Solution 3: Go (Golang)

**Pros:**
- **Performance:** Go is known for its high performance and efficient concurrency model, making it suitable for high-throughput systems.
- **Simplicity:** The language is simple and easy to learn, which can be engaging for developers.
- **Library Support:** Growing ecosystem with libraries for message brokers and web frameworks (e.g., Gin, Echo).
- **Concurrency:** Goroutines provide a powerful concurrency model.

**Cons:**
- **Adoption:** If not already adopted by Xebians, it might require a learning curve.
- **Less Mature Ecosystem:** Compared to Python or JavaScript, the ecosystem is still growing, which might mean fewer libraries for niche use cases.

### Solution 4: Kotlin

**Pros:**
- **Interoperability with Java:** If Xebians have a Java background, Kotlin can be easily adopted due to its interoperability with Java.
- **Modern Language Features:** Offers modern language features that can make development more enjoyable.
- **Library Support:** Can leverage existing Java libraries for message brokers and web frameworks.
- **Performance:** Runs on the JVM, offering good performance.

**Cons:**
- **Adoption:** If not already familiar, it might require some learning effort.
- **Community Size:** Smaller community compared to Python or JavaScript, which might mean fewer resources.

### Best Solution

Considering the decision drivers, **Node.js (JavaScript)** seems to be the best choice. It is likely to be interesting and fun for developers, especially those with front-end experience. It has a strong library support for handling message brokers and building web applications. Additionally, its event-driven architecture is well-suited for the task of handling inbound information through a message broker. While it has some drawbacks, such as potential complexity with callbacks, modern JavaScript features can mitigate these issues. If Xebians are already familiar with JavaScript, the adoption process will be smoother, aligning well with the decision drivers.
While Node.js is indeed a strong contender due to its event-driven architecture and popularity among developers, I would argue that **Python** might be a better choice given the context and decision drivers. Here's why:

### Python as the Best Solution

**1. Familiarity and Adoption:**
- Python is one of the most popular programming languages and is likely already familiar to many developers at Xebians. This familiarity can significantly reduce the learning curve and speed up the development process.

**2. Library Support:**
- Python boasts a vast array of libraries and frameworks that are well-suited for handling message brokers and building web applications. Libraries like Celery for task queues and Django or Flask for web frameworks provide robust solutions for backend development.

**3. Community and Resources:**
- Python's large community means there is a wealth of resources, tutorials, and third-party tools available. This can be particularly beneficial for troubleshooting and finding solutions to common problems.

**4. Ease of Learning and Use:**
- Python's syntax is clean and easy to read, which can make it more engaging and fun for developers. This ease of use can also lead to faster development times and fewer bugs.

**5. Versatility:**
- Python is a versatile language that can be used for a wide range of applications beyond just web development, such as data analysis, machine learning, and automation. This versatility can be an asset if the project scope expands in the future.

**6. Concurrency Improvements:**
- While Python has traditionally been criticized for its concurrency model, recent improvements with asyncio and other libraries have made it more competitive in handling asynchronous tasks.

### Addressing the Cons

- **Performance:** While Python may not be the fastest language, its performance is often sufficient for many web applications, especially when combined with efficient libraries and frameworks.
- **Concurrency:** Python's concurrency capabilities have improved, and for many applications, they are adequate. If high concurrency is a critical requirement, specific libraries and tools can be employed to enhance performance.

### Conclusion

Given the decision drivers, Python's familiarity, extensive library support, and ease of use make it a compelling choice for this project. It aligns well with the goal of being interesting and engaging for developers while also being practical and efficient for the task at hand.

## Context
Within SkyPy all functions used to create a "simulation" will in practice be taking in some values (either parameters or columns from a table) and creating new column(s) in an output table *or* selecting specific rows from an input table.

The inputs and outputs of these functions are clearly defined so a directed acyclic graph (DAG) can be constructed to determine what order the functions should be run in.

To aid in the creation of the tables and the DAG a helper class or decorator should be used so the person writing the function does not have to worry about the implementation details. This class or decorator is what we are currently referring to as the `Model`.

For clarity in the options below we will assume the following example function:
```python
def redshift_gamma(shape, scale, size):
    """Gamma-distributed redshifts (Smail et al. 1994).

    Sample `size` redshifts from a gamma distribution with the
    given `shape` and `scale` parameters. See `numpy.random.gamma`.
    """

    # redshift distribution
    redshift = np.random.gamma(shape=shape, scale=scale, size=size)

    return redshift
```

## Decision Drivers
- Ease of use: if there is too much boiler plate `Model`s will be annoying to write
- Clarity of implementation: the base `Model` should be easy to read, understand, and debug
human_decision:
# ADR 1: Considering options for the SkyPy `Model`
January 22, 2020





## Considered Options

### A base `Model` class
In this implementation all functions must be written inside a class that inherits from the base `Model` class.  A different base class should be used depending on if the function adds a column to a table or selects rows.

The `__init__` method would define all the inputs and outputs and the inherited `__init__` can add this to the DAG.

The `compute` method will contain the custom function.

The `execute` method will call the `compute` method and add the results to the table/mask out rows.

- Ease of use: medium (lots of boiler plate)
- Clarity of implementation: high (Classes are well understood by most developers)

Example:
```python
import BaseModel
import numpy as np

class RedshiftGamma(BaseModel):
    def __init__(self):
        self.inputs = ["shape", "scale", "size"]
        self.outputs = ["redshift"]
        super(RedshiftGamma, self).__init__(self.inputs, self.outputs)
    
    def compute(shape, scale, size):
        """Gamma-distributed redshifts (Smail et al. 1994).

        Sample `size` redshifts from a gamma distribution with the
        given `shape` and `scale` parameters. See `numpy.random.gamma`.
        """

        # redshift distribution
        redshift = np.random.gamma(shape=shape, scale=scale, size=size)

        return redshift
```

### A `Model` decorator
In this implementation all functions must use an `@Model(inputs=[], outputs=[])` decorator. A different decorator should be written for adding columns and selecting rows. The decorator will:

1. Add the `inputs` and `outputs` to the DAG
2. Return a callable function that executes the wrapped function and add the results to the table/mask out rows.

- Ease of use: easy (one line added above a function)
- Clarity of implementation: medium (decorators are functions that return function that return function... This particular implementation will be at least 3 wrappers deep)

Example:
```python
import ModelWrapper
import numpy as np

@ModelWrapper(inputs=["shape", "scale", "size"], outputs=["redshift"])
def redshift_gamma(shape, scale, size):
    """Gamma-distributed redshifts (Smail et al. 1994).

    Sample `size` redshifts from a gamma distribution with the
    given `shape` and `scale` parameters. See `numpy.random.gamma`.
    """

    # redshift distribution
    redshift = np.random.gamma(shape=shape, scale=scale, size=size)

    return redshift
```

### Use the DAG directly
Packages such as [pyungo](https://pypi.org/project/pyungo/) have APIs for most of the functionality we need here with decorators that define `inputs` and `outputs`.  When the compute graph is called all `inputs` and `outputs` are stored in the returned `results` data structure.  Once computed we can write a function that turns this into the final data table.

Also the actual function wrapping only needs to happen for functions contained in the configuration file preventing any un-needed nodes being added to the graph.

We kind of get masking for free here as the DAG does not care if/when the number of rows changes, we just have to be careful when constructing the final table out of the `results`.

- Ease of use: easy (one line added above a function)
- Clarity of implementation: high (we off load this to an existing package that we don't have to maintain)

Example:
```python
def redshift_gamma(shape, scale, size):
    """Gamma-distributed redshifts (Smail et al. 1994).

    Sample `size` redshifts from a gamma distribution with the
    given `shape` and `scale` parameters. See `numpy.random.gamma`.
    """

    # redshift distribution
    redshift = np.random.gamma(shape=shape, scale=scale, size=size)

    return redshift
```

After reading in the config we can wrap all the functions that we need:
```python
from pyungo import Graph

graph = Graph()
graph.register()(redshift_gamma)
res = graph.calculate(data={'shape': 1, 'scale': 1, 'size': 5})
# res is a dict with `shape`, `scale`, `size`, and `redshift`
# this dict can be turned into a table
```

## Decision Outcome
After [discussion](https://github.com/skypyproject/skypy/pull/38) option 3 has been picked.  This will be easiest for developers to write new functions and write clean unit tests.  Within the example given above `pyungo` was just used as an example, other DAG frameworks exist and picking one should be the topic of a different ADR.

message_history:
To address the problem of creating a helper class or decorator for managing the implementation details of functions in SkyPy, we can consider several solutions. Each solution will be evaluated based on ease of use and clarity of implementation.

### Solution 1: Use a Decorator

**Description**: Implement a decorator that automatically handles the input and output table management, as well as the DAG construction.

**Pros**:
- **Ease of Use**: Decorators can be applied directly to functions, minimizing boilerplate code. Users can focus on writing the core logic of their functions.
- **Clarity**: The decorator can encapsulate complex logic, making the function implementation cleaner and more readable.
- **Flexibility**: Decorators can be easily modified or extended to add additional functionality without changing the function's core logic.

**Cons**:
- **Debugging Complexity**: Debugging can be more challenging because the decorator abstracts away some of the logic.
- **Learning Curve**: Users unfamiliar with decorators may need to learn how they work, which could be a barrier to entry.

### Solution 2: Use a Helper Class

**Description**: Create a base `Model` class that users can inherit from. This class would handle input/output management and DAG construction.

**Pros**:
- **Clarity**: The class structure can provide a clear and organized way to manage inputs and outputs, making it easier to understand the flow of data.
- **Extensibility**: Users can extend the base class to add custom functionality or override existing methods.
- **Debugging**: Easier to debug compared to decorators, as the logic is more explicit.

**Cons**:
- **Boilerplate Code**: Users may need to write more boilerplate code to set up the class and its methods.
- **Complexity**: The class structure might be more complex to understand and use, especially for simple functions.

### Solution 3: Use a Configuration File

**Description**: Define the inputs, outputs, and DAG structure in a separate configuration file (e.g., YAML or JSON). The functions would then be linked to this configuration.

**Pros**:
- **Separation of Concerns**: Keeps the function logic separate from the configuration, making both easier to manage.
- **Flexibility**: Configuration files can be easily modified without changing the codebase.
- **Clarity**: The configuration file provides a clear overview of the entire simulation setup.

**Cons**:
- **Complexity**: Managing and synchronizing the configuration file with the code can be complex.
- **Ease of Use**: Users need to understand the configuration format and how it links to the code, which could be cumbersome.

### Best Solution

Considering the decision drivers of ease of use and clarity of implementation, the **decorator approach** seems to be the best solution. It minimizes boilerplate code, keeps the function implementation clean, and encapsulates complex logic. While it may introduce some debugging challenges, the benefits of ease of use and flexibility outweigh these drawbacks. Additionally, decorators can be designed to provide clear error messages and logging to aid in debugging, mitigating some of the potential downsides.
While the decorator approach has its merits, I would argue that the **helper class approach** might be a more suitable solution for the SkyPy context, given the decision drivers of ease of use and clarity of implementation.

### Reasons for Choosing the Helper Class Approach

1. **Explicit Structure and Organization**:
   - A helper class provides a clear and organized structure for managing inputs, outputs, and DAG construction. This explicitness can make it easier for users to understand the flow of data and the relationships between different components of the simulation.

2. **Ease of Debugging**:
   - Unlike decorators, which can abstract away logic and make debugging more challenging, a class-based approach allows for more explicit and transparent logic. This can make it easier to trace errors and understand the behavior of the code.

3. **Extensibility and Customization**:
   - A class-based approach allows users to extend the base class to add custom functionality or override existing methods. This can be particularly useful in a scientific context where users may have specific requirements or need to implement custom behavior.

4. **Reduced Learning Curve**:
   - While decorators are powerful, they can introduce a learning curve for users unfamiliar with them. A class-based approach, on the other hand, relies on object-oriented programming principles, which are widely understood and used in many programming contexts.

5. **Consistency and Reusability**:
   - A helper class can provide a consistent interface for all functions, making it easier to reuse and maintain code. This consistency can also aid in collaboration and code sharing among users.

### Addressing the Cons

- **Boilerplate Code**: While a class-based approach may require more boilerplate code, this can be mitigated by providing a well-documented base class with clear examples and templates for common use cases.
- **Complexity**: The complexity of the class structure can be managed by providing comprehensive documentation and support for users, as well as by designing the class to be as intuitive and user-friendly as possible.

In conclusion, while the decorator approach offers ease of use and flexibility, the helper class approach provides a more explicit, organized, and extensible solution that aligns well with the needs of scientific simulations in SkyPy. The benefits of clarity, ease of debugging, and extensibility make it a strong candidate for managing the implementation details of functions in this context.

## Context and Problem Statement

The [Settings Redesign project](https://github.com/mozilla/fxa/issues/3740) offers an ideal opportunity to review CSS conventions and architecture for FxA to not only use while building new components for this project, but also for FxA moving forward.

This [part 1](https://github.com/mozilla/fxa/issues/4808) of [2](https://github.com/mozilla/fxa/issues/5087) ADR concerns the use of CSS variables, how they come into play with SCSS (vs using SCSS variables), CSS-in-JS considerations, and CSS modules.

Note that 1) "CSS variables" are technically called "CSS custom properties" but will be referred to as the common "variable" nomenclature throughout this ADR and that 2) SASS is a CSS preprocessor while SCSS is our preferred syntax of SASS (explained [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/5654447/whats-the-difference-between-scss-and-sass/5654471#5654471)).

## Decision Drivers

- Simplicity and clarity
- DRY and reusable styles
- Easy integration and use
- Current engineering experience and preferences
human_decision:
# Use CSS Variables, Prefer SCSS over CSS-in-JS

- Deciders: Lauren Zugai, Jody Heavener
- Date: 2020-04-30





## Considered Options

### Styling variables

- SASS variables
- CSS variables
- A combination of SASS and CSS variables

### Styling placement

- CSS-in-JS with a helper lib like [`styled-components`](https://styled-components.com/) or [`react-jss`](https://cssinjs.org/react-jss/)
- CSS modules
- SCSS

## Decision Outcome

Chosen options: "CSS variables" and "SCSS", because:

- While using CSS variables with SASS will prevent using mixins (or SASS functions like `darken`) that rely on variable values, they can be used without impeding core functionality FxA uses SASS for like nested selectors, extends, and functions and mixins that don't rely on variable values like the generation and use of our media queries.
- CSS variables are native to the browser and decision to use them now is a prudent one. They have good browser support and can be used without a preprocessor and changed after preprocessor compilation. They are targetable by JavaScript, easily themed, and can be scoped globally or on an element, providing options for usage.
- Scoped component solutions (CSS-in-JS, CSS modules) encourage self-contained components rather than building the UI as a whole. While one goal of FxA is to reuse components across the ecosystem where possible (e.g. the `fxa-components` package), FxA will likely reap more benefits from a class-based approach. This allows for globally shared styles and a loosely coupled stylesheet rather than a tightly coupled CSS-in-JS solution with conditional styles based on props and how the component is used. Classes promote a DRYer and more consistent approach.
- CSS-in-JS would add one additional layer of tech to learn (like `styled-components` syntax and best practices) while the Settings Redesign project is already introducing other novel tech, and members of the FxA engineering team also as a whole personally prefer not to use CSS-in-JS.
- This decision doesn't preclude the option of implementing CSS modules at a later time if it's determined that we would prefer component scoped styles with SCSS, and CSS variables could still be used if we later switch to CSS-in-JS.

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### Styling variables

#### SASS variables

[SASS variables](https://sass-lang.com/documentation/variables) refer to CSS values set to a name, such as `$font-size`, in SASS files that can not only be referenced after their declaration but can also be used to utilize many of SASS's powerful features. They are compiled at runtime and do not exist in compiled CSS output.

Pros:

- SASS variables have been around for some time and have established best organizational practices.
- Using SASS variables would allow usage of more advanced SASS functions like calculations, functions like `darken`, and directives like `@if ($some-variable >= 14px)`.

Neutral:

- SASS variables are imperative and can only hold one value at a time so if the variable changes in value, earlier references will remain the same.
- We'd need to add to our `rescripts` webpack custom config to expose global SASS variables to all of our files.

Cons:

- SASS variables offer a limited set of "pre-compilation" power because variables can't be set dynamically after the stylesheet has been loaded.

#### CSS variables

[CSS custom properties](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/Using_CSS_custom_properties), or CSS variables, provide a powerful native browser solution to setting CSS properties to variables. They are declared as `--font-color: grey` and used as `color: var(--font-color)` and are present in outputted CSS after SASS compilation.

Pros:

- We can still use many SASS features with CSS variables. We will still be able to nest selectors, use mixins that don't require calculations based on variable values such as our responsive design mixins (like `@include min-width('tablet')`) and other mixins with the purpose of shared styles for DRY'ing up our code.
- Choosing CSS variables helps to "future proof" FxA's front-end stack. Where SASS allows function execution on SASS variables, down the line we will be able to use CSS functions, such as color modifiers, on native CSS variables.
- CSS variables are easy to target with JavaScript when necessary and unlock new possibilities - while we have chosen to prefer CSS in SCSS files and not in JS (see the second decision made in this ADR), it does open the possibility of updating variable values if the use case arises.
- CSS variables are ideal for theming and will make a future "dark mode" feature requested by UX very straightforward, as we would simply change a set of variables either in a `prefers-color-scheme` media query or by checking a flag set in localStorage.
- CSS variables can be global or scoped to an element/selector. For example, variable values can change per class without having to set the property again:

```
h2 {
  --h2-color: grey;
  color: var(--h2-color);
}

.special h2 {
  --h2-color: black;
}
```

Neutral:

- While CSS variables meet the minimum browser compatibility list for FxA and have [great support otherwise](https://caniuse.com/#feat=css-variables), they aren't supported in IE11.
- CSS variables can be seen in developer tools. If there are many scoped to `:root` (globally) or the component in question, it can look like a lot of cruft, but this can also be convenient depending on what is being inspected.

Cons

- Since SASS functions, mixins, etc. are ran at runtime and CSS variables are dynamic, some SASS functionality is incompatible. However, FxA does not currently use SASS functions like `darken()` so the transition should be relatively painless, and we can still use the native CSS `calc()` property.
- Because CSS variables are fairly new, best practices and patterns are still being established. While some opinions have been formed based on work from the `fxa-admin-panel` and other small projects both in and outside work for Mozilla, we'll need to make some architectural decisions in part 2 of our CSS ADRs based on this experience and what we believe will pave the best way forward for FxA.

Small win: the Bento menu from the Monitor site, which we will be implementing in the Settings Redesign project, uses CSS variables.

### A combination of SASS and CSS variables

Static values (colors, font-sizes) could be declared as SASS variables while other values could be set as CSS variables.

Pros:

- Using both variable types would allow for calculations in mixins where needed from static values.
- It would be very clear what variables should never change.
- If implemented correctly and maintained, could take the best of both worlds between CSS variables and advanced SASS functionality.

Cons:

- This approach is unconventional and is likely to be confusing for current and new team members, plus open source contributors, without reading through FxA CSS stack documentation. Engineers may unknowingly set a variable as a SASS variable when it should be a CSS variable and vice versa. If some mixins can only be used with certain variables, it can lead to additional debugging for someone not acquainted with this decision. This goes against our desire for simplicity and clarity.

--

### Styling placement

### CSS-in-JS with a helper lib like `styled-components` or `react-jss`

CSS-in-JS solutions blend CSS into JavaScript, scope styles to the component, and typically live in the same file as the component declaration. Depending on the library, the syntax can resemble CSS or actually look quite different.

Pros:

- CSS-in-JS would allow us to take advantage of state-based styling without conditional class names, and instead, would set conditional styles.
- Would allow for easy deletion and maintenance of styles. If a component is deleted or moved, so are all of the related styles. Engineers could modify components with confidence that other components wouldn't be affected.
- We could still use CSS variables.
- Many CSS-in-JS libraries integrate well with React and `styled-components` is quickly growing in popularity.
- Since styles are scoped per component, generated CSS class names won't clash with each other as they are unique, and specificity-wise will be short and efficient. Engineers would not have to come up with a name for each class.

Neutral:

- Introduces less separation of concern between styles and components plus a more JS-like syntax for our CSS. This can be a pro or con depending on individual preference.

Cons:

- Since styles are scoped per component, it can lead to copypasta, less shared styles, and less consistency between components.
- We would be unable to use SASS features like mixins, causing us to reevaluate how we'd set our media queries and potentially other heavily relied on aspects of our current SCSS implementation.
- A library adds one more dependency layer to our stack, as well as one more piece of technology that our current and future team members would need to learn, and one more hurdle for open source contributors especially since CSS fixes are a common contribution. We would need to adjust to new nesting syntax and other conventions.
- Sharing styles between components is more straightforward in `styled-components` than `react-jss`, but is still more complex than adding a class name to reference shared styles.
- At least several team members have expressed a dislike for CSS-in-JS for various reasons.
- A minor inconvenience, but we would need to setup yet another linting file (a root-level `.stylelint`).

### CSS modules

[CSS modules](https://css-tricks.com/css-modules-part-1-need/) could help bridge the gap between an external stylesheet and CSS-in-JS as they allow for CSS in an external stylesheet while scoping styles to a component.

Pros:

- Can be used with SCSS, _and_ CSS or SASS variables.
- Can be viewed as "SASS with scoped classes." We could still get most of the SCSS benefits by using SCSS and setting classes for shared styles while benefitting from scoped component generated class names to prevent name clashes and relieve the need to manually name these classes.

Cons:

- Typical use would be to scope all styles for a component to that component, which would inherit the potential copypasta, less shared styles, and less consistency between components problem of CSS-in-JS. If we chose to use classes for shared styles and only scope unique component styles, simplicity and clarity could be muddled.
- Less separation of concern than the SCSS only option (but greater than CSS-in-JS), as it requires setting the styles on React components inline in the JSX.
- A combination of shared classes and scoped inline styles could make debugging CSS issues slightly more complex.
- Similar to CSS-in-JS but on a lesser scale, it would introduce yet another new frontend concept for our engineers to learn and understand.

### SCSS

[SASS](https://sass-lang.com/guide) is a CSS preposessor that converts external `.sass` or `.scss` files with variables, nesting, mixins, inheritence, and other conveniences into pure CSS. SCSS is a syntax flavor of SASS preferred by many.

Pros:

- The team is already familiar with and likes SCSS, and has expressed a preference of external styles over CSS-in-JS.
- SCSS is still a very popular choice for CSS organization and is included as a dependency in `create-react-app` by default.
- Encourages building the UI as a whole with shared styles instead of component encapsulation. While this could be a "con" in large projects with many shared components, FxA will have a lot of global shared styles and an SCSS class name based approach will result DRYer code.
- Offers handy functionality that we can still use with CSS variables like nesting and mixins.

Cons:

- Not as clear as some other solutions what styles are being applied per component without potentially checking multiple files.
- Requires naming convention standards for class names and organization to ensure easy maintenance.

## Links

- [Settings Redesign epic](https://github.com/mozilla/fxa/issues/3740)
- [CSS ADR part 1 GH issue](https://github.com/mozilla/fxa/issues/4808), [CSS ADR part 2 GH issue](https://github.com/mozilla/fxa/issues/5087)
- [`styled-components`](https://styled-components.com/), [`react-jss`](https://cssinjs.org/react-jss/)
- [SASS](https://sass-lang.com/guide), [SASS variables](https://sass-lang.com/documentation/variables), [SASS vs SCSS](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/5654447/whats-the-difference-between-scss-and-sass/5654471#5654471)
- [CSS custom properties/variables](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/Using_CSS_custom_properties)
- [CSS variables caniuse](https://caniuse.com/#feat=css-variables)
- [CSS modules](https://css-tricks.com/css-modules-part-1-need/)
debate_answer: The best solution is to use SCSS with CSS Variables, as it combines the strengths of both approaches and aligns well with the team's current experience and project goals.
reason: The negative side's proposal to use SCSS with CSS Variables aligns more closely with the decision drivers, particularly the current engineering experience and preferences. This approach leverages the team's existing familiarity with SCSS while introducing the flexibility of CSS Variables for dynamic theming. It offers a balanced solution that addresses both compile-time and runtime needs, making it a more practical choice for the team.
message_history:
To address the problem statement and decision drivers, we can consider several solutions for CSS conventions and architecture in the Settings Redesign project. Here are some potential solutions, along with their pros and cons:

### Solution 1: Use CSS Variables (Custom Properties)

**Pros:**
- **Dynamic Theming:** CSS variables allow for easy theming and runtime changes without recompiling styles.
- **Native Support:** They are supported natively by modern browsers, reducing the need for additional tooling.
- **Inheritance:** CSS variables can be inherited, making it easier to manage styles across components.

**Cons:**
- **Browser Compatibility:** While modern browsers support CSS variables, older browsers may not, requiring fallbacks.
- **Complexity in Large Projects:** Managing a large number of CSS variables can become complex and hard to track.

### Solution 2: Use SCSS Variables

**Pros:**
- **Compile-Time Evaluation:** SCSS variables are evaluated at compile time, which can lead to more optimized CSS output.
- **Familiarity:** Many developers are already familiar with SCSS, making it easier to integrate into existing workflows.
- **Tooling Support:** SCSS has strong tooling support, including mixins and functions, which can enhance productivity.

**Cons:**
- **Lack of Runtime Flexibility:** SCSS variables cannot be changed at runtime, limiting dynamic theming capabilities.
- **Additional Build Step:** Requires a build step to compile SCSS into CSS, which can add complexity to the development process.

### Solution 3: CSS-in-JS

**Pros:**
- **Component-Scoped Styles:** CSS-in-JS allows for styles to be scoped to components, reducing the risk of style conflicts.
- **Dynamic Styling:** Styles can be dynamically generated based on component props or state.
- **Integration with JavaScript:** Seamless integration with JavaScript logic, making it easier to manage styles in complex applications.

**Cons:**
- **Performance Overhead:** Can introduce runtime performance overhead due to style generation.
- **Learning Curve:** May require developers to learn new libraries or paradigms, such as styled-components or Emotion.
- **Tooling and Ecosystem:** The ecosystem is still evolving, and there may be less mature tooling compared to traditional CSS/SCSS.

### Solution 4: CSS Modules

**Pros:**
- **Scoped Styles:** CSS Modules automatically scope styles to components, reducing global namespace pollution.
- **Familiar Syntax:** Uses standard CSS syntax, making it easy for developers to adopt.
- **Integration with Build Tools:** Works well with modern build tools like Webpack, providing a seamless development experience.

**Cons:**
- **Configuration Required:** Requires configuration in the build process, which can add complexity.
- **Limited Dynamic Capabilities:** While CSS Modules provide scoping, they do not inherently support dynamic styling like CSS-in-JS.

### Selecting the Best Solution

Considering the decision drivers of simplicity, clarity, DRY and reusable styles, easy integration, and current engineering experience, the best solution might be a combination of CSS Variables and CSS Modules:

- **CSS Variables** provide the flexibility and dynamic theming capabilities needed for modern web applications, while also being simple and clear to use.
- **CSS Modules** offer scoped styles, reducing the risk of conflicts and maintaining clarity in the codebase.

This combination leverages the strengths of both approaches, providing a robust and flexible styling solution that aligns with the project's goals and the team's existing experience.
While the combination of CSS Variables and CSS Modules is a strong choice, I propose an alternative approach that might better align with the decision drivers, particularly focusing on the current engineering experience and preferences.

### Alternative Solution: Use SCSS with CSS Variables

**Rationale:**
- **Familiarity and Integration:** Given that the team is already using SCSS, continuing with SCSS variables ensures a smooth transition and leverages existing knowledge and tooling. This aligns with the decision driver of current engineering experience.
- **Dynamic Theming with CSS Variables:** By integrating CSS Variables within SCSS, we can achieve dynamic theming capabilities. CSS Variables can be used for properties that need runtime flexibility, while SCSS variables can handle compile-time constants.
- **Enhanced Tooling and Productivity:** SCSS provides powerful features like mixins, functions, and nesting, which can enhance productivity and maintain DRY principles. This can be combined with CSS Variables for theming and runtime changes.

**Pros:**
- **Leverages Existing Knowledge:** The team can continue using familiar SCSS syntax and tools, reducing the learning curve and integration challenges.
- **Balanced Flexibility:** Combines the compile-time benefits of SCSS with the runtime flexibility of CSS Variables, offering a balanced approach.
- **Optimized Output:** SCSS's compile-time evaluation can lead to more optimized CSS output, improving performance.

**Cons:**
- **Complexity in Management:** Managing both SCSS and CSS Variables can introduce complexity, requiring clear documentation and conventions.
- **Build Process Dependency:** Still requires a build step for SCSS, which can add complexity to the development process.

### Conclusion

This alternative solution leverages the strengths of both SCSS and CSS Variables, providing a familiar and powerful toolset for the team while introducing the flexibility needed for modern web applications. It aligns well with the decision drivers, particularly in terms of current engineering experience and preferences, while also addressing the need for dynamic theming and DRY principles.
