## Context and Problem Statement

We want to **deploy** pods, services, and other resources on **Kubernetes**,
which is a GKE cluster for now.

What tool should we use to do this?

## Decision Drivers

- Ease of use
- How "far" is the tool from the Kubernetes lifecycle? - _"Is it trailing
  after upstream and if so, how far away from it"_
- Ability to express deployments in a descriptive "as-code" way
- Ease of use in a CI/CD pipeline - _Does it require a local state? Weird
  requirements? Auth to other systems?_
- Reliability - _"Will the tool usage often lead to messed up
  services/cluster?"_
human_decision:
# Use kubectl and Helm for Kubernetes deployment

- Status: Accepted
- Date: 2019-03-17
- Deciders:
    - achotard
    - jmartinsanchez
    - ocloirec





## Considered Options

- [Terraform](#terraform)
- [Ansible](#ansible)
- [Kubectl](#kubectl)
- [Helm](#helm)

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: "A mix of **kubectl** and **Helm**", because:

- `kubectl` is the native way of deploying to Kubernetes and it thus the most
  documented, widely-used, appropriate and up-to-date.
- Helm fits best for complex stacks deployment and we're eager to try things
  such as Helm charts repositories!

Helm will be used as an alternative to `kubectl` in a second part, when we'll
ramp up things and when we will need to deploy more complex stacks. The
beginning will be `kubectl` for our own services, and Helm for
components/middleware/databases with existing charts such as RabbitMQ!

This imply that the provided Kubernetes cluster(s) for this project will need
to be **provisionned with Helm requirements such as Tiller**.

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### Terraform

[Terraform](https://www.terraform.io/) is an infrastructure-as-code tool
developed by [Hashicorp](https://www.hashicorp.com/) that has support for a
[Kubernetes
provider](https://www.terraform.io/docs/providers/kubernetes/index.html).

Pros:

- Already used to spawn the underlying infrastructure on GCP (Projects, GKE
  cluster, etc)

Cons:

- Is yet another format overlay to describe Kubernetes resources
- Requires useless state synchronization

### Ansible

[Ansible](https://www.ansible.com/) and its [Kubernetes
modules](https://docs.ansible.com/ansible/latest/modules/kubernetes_module.html)
were a **No-GO** from the beginning since we're trying to avoid using Ansible
for unappropriate use cases such as this one.  Talking to APIs such as the
Kubernetes one is definitely not the job for which Ansible was made for.

### Kubectl

[`kubectl`](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/overview/) is the
official command line tool to talk to the Kubernetes API.

Pros:

- Official tooling
- The GCP `gcloud` CLI allow easy authentication of `kubectl` to the Kubernetes
  API thanks to the `gcloud container clusters get-credentials  <cluster_name>
  --region <cluster_region>` command.

Cons:

- May be a bit harsh to deploy more complex stacks, and thus lead to CD
  pipelines with many many steps

### Helm

[Helm](https://helm.sh/) is _"the package manager for Kubernetes"_. It can be
seen as a way to package Kubernetes manifests and template them in order to
"bundle" complete descriptions. It uses what are called [_**Helm
charts**_](https://helm.sh/docs/glossary/#chart) to describe these bundles, and
these Helm charts can then be deployed on Kubernetes with `helm install`. Helm
requires a component called [`Tiller`](https://helm.sh/docs/glossary/#tiller)
to be installed on the Kubernetes cluster.

Pros:

- Community-agreed tooling with a widespread use
- Existing Helm charts for many open source software such as RabbitMQ which we
  are planning to use (see
  [`rabbitmq`](https://github.com/helm/charts/tree/master/stable/rabbitmq) and
  [`rabbitmq-ha`](https://github.com/helm/charts/tree/master/stable/rabbitmq-ha)
  **stable** Helm charts)
- Better revision & lifecycle handling than raw `kubectl`

Cons:

- Has some painful limitation according to some feedback we got
- Need to setup Tiller on the K8s clusters
- Existing charts often lack documentation which make it a bit painful to tune

The main challenge with Helm will be to pre-provision the GKE clusters with
Helm in a clean way, ideally not running by `helm init` but in a more "as-code"
approach.

## Links

- <https://dzone.com/articles/terraform-vs-helm-for-kubernetes> has been useful
  to transmit a more concrete insight on Terraform vs Helm.
- [Official Helm charts](https://github.com/helm/charts)
debate_answer: ArgoCD is the best solution for deploying resources on a Kubernetes GKE cluster, as it provides a reliable, efficient, and modern approach to managing deployments.
reason: The negative side presents a compelling argument for ArgoCD, emphasizing its alignment with modern DevOps practices through a GitOps approach. This method offers a clear advantage in terms of ease of use, lifecycle proximity, and reliability, particularly in maintaining the desired state of the cluster. While Helm is a strong candidate, ArgoCD's ability to integrate seamlessly with Git-based workflows and reduce deployment complexity makes it a more suitable choice given the decision drivers.
message_history:
To address the problem of deploying resources on a Kubernetes GKE cluster, several tools can be considered. Here are some potential solutions, along with their pros and cons:

### 1. **kubectl**

**Pros:**
- **Ease of Use:** Directly interacts with Kubernetes API, making it straightforward for those familiar with Kubernetes.
- **Lifecycle Proximity:** Always up-to-date with the latest Kubernetes features as it is the default CLI tool.
- **Descriptive "as-code" Deployments:** Supports YAML configuration files, which are standard for Kubernetes.
- **CI/CD Integration:** Can be easily integrated into CI/CD pipelines with minimal setup.
- **Reliability:** Highly reliable as it is the native tool for Kubernetes.

**Cons:**
- **Complexity for Large Deployments:** Can become cumbersome for managing complex deployments without additional tooling.
- **Manual Management:** Requires manual scripting for automation, which can lead to errors.

### 2. **Helm**

**Pros:**
- **Ease of Use:** Simplifies deployment with charts, which package Kubernetes resources.
- **Lifecycle Proximity:** Actively maintained and closely follows Kubernetes updates.
- **Descriptive "as-code" Deployments:** Uses Helm charts, which are templated YAML files.
- **CI/CD Integration:** Well-supported in CI/CD environments, with many plugins and integrations available.
- **Reliability:** Widely used and tested, reducing the likelihood of errors.

**Cons:**
- **Learning Curve:** Requires learning Helm-specific templating and commands.
- **State Management:** Manages its own state, which can complicate deployments if not handled properly.

### 3. **Terraform with Kubernetes Provider**

**Pros:**
- **Ease of Use:** Provides a consistent workflow for managing infrastructure and Kubernetes resources.
- **Descriptive "as-code" Deployments:** Uses HashiCorp Configuration Language (HCL), which is clear and concise.
- **CI/CD Integration:** Well-suited for CI/CD pipelines, especially if Terraform is already in use for other infrastructure.
- **Reliability:** Strong community support and regular updates.

**Cons:**
- **Lifecycle Proximity:** May lag behind Kubernetes updates as it relies on the Kubernetes provider.
- **State Management:** Requires managing Terraform state, which can be complex.

### 4. **ArgoCD**

**Pros:**
- **Ease of Use:** Provides a GitOps approach, making deployments straightforward once set up.
- **Lifecycle Proximity:** Actively developed and follows Kubernetes updates closely.
- **Descriptive "as-code" Deployments:** Uses Git repositories as the source of truth for configurations.
- **CI/CD Integration:** Seamlessly integrates with Git-based workflows.
- **Reliability:** Ensures that the cluster state matches the desired state in Git, reducing drift.

**Cons:**
- **Initial Setup Complexity:** Can be complex to set up initially.
- **Learning Curve:** Requires understanding of GitOps principles.

### Best Solution

Considering the decision drivers, **Helm** emerges as the best solution. It strikes a balance between ease of use, lifecycle proximity, and reliability. Helm's templating system allows for descriptive "as-code" deployments, and its integration with CI/CD pipelines is well-supported. While it does have a learning curve, the benefits of using Helm, especially for managing complex deployments, outweigh the initial setup challenges. Additionally, Helm's active community and regular updates ensure it remains aligned with Kubernetes advancements.
While Helm is indeed a strong candidate for deploying resources on a Kubernetes GKE cluster, I would argue that **ArgoCD** might be a more suitable choice given the decision drivers outlined. Here's why:

### ArgoCD as the Best Solution

**1. Ease of Use:**
   - ArgoCD provides a GitOps approach, which is increasingly popular for its simplicity and efficiency. Once set up, it allows for straightforward management of deployments by using Git as the single source of truth. This can significantly reduce the complexity of managing deployments compared to Helm, which requires understanding its templating system.

**2. Lifecycle Proximity:**
   - ArgoCD is actively developed and closely follows Kubernetes updates. Its GitOps model ensures that the cluster state is always in sync with the desired state defined in Git, which can be more intuitive and less error-prone than managing Helm charts.

**3. Descriptive "as-code" Deployments:**
   - By using Git repositories to store configuration files, ArgoCD naturally supports "as-code" deployments. This aligns well with modern DevOps practices and provides a clear audit trail of changes.

**4. Ease of Use in a CI/CD Pipeline:**
   - ArgoCD integrates seamlessly with Git-based workflows, making it an excellent fit for CI/CD pipelines. It eliminates the need for managing local state or complex authentication setups, which can be a challenge with Helm or Terraform.

**5. Reliability:**
   - ArgoCD's model of continuously ensuring that the live state matches the desired state in Git reduces the risk of drift and errors. This reliability is crucial for maintaining stable and predictable deployments.

### Addressing the Cons

- **Initial Setup Complexity:** While ArgoCD can be complex to set up initially, the long-term benefits of a GitOps approach, such as reduced manual intervention and increased deployment consistency, can outweigh the initial setup challenges.
  
- **Learning Curve:** Understanding GitOps principles is necessary, but this is becoming a standard practice in the industry, and the learning investment can pay off in terms of operational efficiency and reliability.

### Conclusion

Given the decision drivers, ArgoCD offers a robust solution that aligns well with modern DevOps practices. Its GitOps approach provides a clear, reliable, and efficient way to manage Kubernetes deployments, making it a compelling choice for deploying resources on a GKE cluster. While Helm is a strong contender, the advantages of ArgoCD in terms of ease of use, lifecycle proximity, and reliability make it the best solution in this context.

## Context and Problem Statement

What protocol(s) shall we use as the new standard for authentication and authorization.
It would be supported by our new centralized authority server and should be implemented in all new clients and
resource systems needing authentication or authorization. A requirement might be made that the authority service need
to support other protocols for legacy systems but all new systems should be encourage to use the same protocol.

## Decision Drivers

- Secure
- Well defined and well reviewed standard
- Easy to implement by client and resource systems
- Support for non web client systems i.e. mobile devices
human_decision:
# Use OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect as protocols for Authentication and Authorization

- Status: accepted
- Date: 2020-06-02





## Considered Options

- OAuth 2.0 + OpenID Connect
- SAML 2.0

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: "OAuth 2.0 + OpenID Connect", because it is secure and well
examined and and has support libraries for our tech stack.

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### OAuth 2.0 + OpenID Connect

- Good, because the authentication protocal is designed specifically to work with the authorization protocol.
- Good, because it supports non web clients i.e. native apps.
- Good, because it has certified, open source libraries for relying parties for OpenID authentication that match our
  tech stack (javascript with typescript defenitions).
- Bad, because it could require large tokens for authorization for multiple services, or split up tokens complicating
  the process.

### SAML 2.0

- Good, because it is the currently used standard for legacy systems.
- Bad, because it doesn't have good support for non web clients.
- Bad, because main focus is on enterprise SSO, not centralized authorization.
debate_answer: OpenID Connect is the best choice for the new standard for authentication and authorization, as it provides a secure, well-reviewed, and comprehensive solution that supports mobile devices and future needs.
reason: The affirmative side presents a compelling case for OpenID Connect by highlighting its additional security features and identity layer built on top of OAuth 2.0. This makes it a more comprehensive solution for both authentication and authorization, aligning well with the decision drivers of security and support for mobile devices. The widespread adoption and future-proof nature of OpenID Connect further strengthen its position as the best choice.
message_history:
To address the problem statement, we need to evaluate several authentication and authorization protocols that meet the decision drivers. Here are some potential solutions:

### 1. OAuth 2.0

**Pros:**
- **Secure:** OAuth 2.0 is widely recognized for its security features, including token-based authentication, which reduces the risk of credential exposure.
- **Well Defined and Reviewed:** It is a well-established standard with extensive documentation and community support.
- **Ease of Implementation:** Many libraries and frameworks support OAuth 2.0, making it easier to implement across different platforms.
- **Support for Mobile Devices:** OAuth 2.0 is designed to work well with mobile applications, providing a seamless user experience.

**Cons:**
- **Complexity:** The protocol can be complex to implement correctly, especially when dealing with different grant types and scopes.
- **Security Concerns:** If not implemented correctly, OAuth 2.0 can be vulnerable to certain attacks, such as token interception.

### 2. OpenID Connect

**Pros:**
- **Secure:** Built on top of OAuth 2.0, it adds an identity layer, providing additional security features.
- **Well Defined and Reviewed:** It is a widely adopted standard with strong community support.
- **Ease of Implementation:** OpenID Connect simplifies the process of obtaining user identity information, making it easier for developers.
- **Support for Mobile Devices:** It is designed to work seamlessly with mobile and web applications.

**Cons:**
- **Complexity:** Like OAuth 2.0, it can be complex to implement, especially for developers unfamiliar with the protocol.
- **Overhead:** It may introduce additional overhead compared to simpler authentication methods.

### 3. SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language)

**Pros:**
- **Secure:** SAML is a mature protocol with strong security features, widely used in enterprise environments.
- **Well Defined and Reviewed:** It has been extensively reviewed and is a well-established standard.
- **Interoperability:** SAML is known for its ability to integrate with various systems, making it suitable for legacy support.

**Cons:**
- **Complexity:** SAML can be complex to implement and maintain, especially for smaller organizations.
- **Not Mobile-Friendly:** It is primarily designed for web-based applications and may not be the best fit for mobile devices.

### 4. FIDO2/WebAuthn

**Pros:**
- **Secure:** FIDO2 provides strong security through passwordless authentication, reducing the risk of phishing and credential theft.
- **Well Defined and Reviewed:** It is a modern standard with growing adoption and support.
- **Ease of Implementation:** Many platforms and browsers support FIDO2, simplifying implementation.
- **Support for Mobile Devices:** FIDO2 is designed to work across various devices, including mobile.

**Cons:**
- **Adoption:** While growing, FIDO2 is not as widely adopted as OAuth 2.0 or SAML, which may limit compatibility.
- **Hardware Requirements:** It may require specific hardware (e.g., security keys) for full functionality.

### Selecting the Best Solution

Considering the decision drivers, **OpenID Connect** appears to be the best choice. It builds on the strengths of OAuth 2.0, providing a secure and well-reviewed standard with strong support for mobile devices. While it may introduce some complexity, the benefits of a unified identity layer and ease of obtaining user information make it a compelling choice for new systems. Additionally, its widespread adoption and support make it a future-proof solution for authentication and authorization needs.
While OpenID Connect is indeed a strong contender, I would argue that **OAuth 2.0** itself could be the best choice for the following reasons:

### Reasons for Choosing OAuth 2.0

1. **Flexibility and Customization:**
   - OAuth 2.0 provides a flexible framework that can be tailored to meet specific needs. It supports various grant types (authorization code, implicit, client credentials, and resource owner password credentials), allowing for customization based on the application's requirements.

2. **Wide Adoption and Ecosystem:**
   - OAuth 2.0 is one of the most widely adopted protocols for authorization, with a vast ecosystem of libraries and tools available for different programming languages and platforms. This makes it easier for developers to find resources and support.

3. **Focus on Authorization:**
   - While OpenID Connect adds an identity layer, OAuth 2.0 focuses on authorization, which might be more aligned with the needs of systems that primarily require access control rather than identity verification.

4. **Simplicity for Certain Use Cases:**
   - For applications that do not require the additional identity features of OpenID Connect, OAuth 2.0 can be simpler to implement and manage, reducing overhead.

5. **Support for Mobile and Non-Web Clients:**
   - OAuth 2.0 is designed to work well with mobile applications and other non-web clients, providing a seamless experience across different device types.

### Addressing the Cons

- **Complexity:** While OAuth 2.0 can be complex, this is often due to the need to understand its various components and flows. With proper documentation and training, this complexity can be managed effectively.
  
- **Security Concerns:** Implementing OAuth 2.0 securely requires adherence to best practices, such as using HTTPS, validating tokens, and implementing proper token storage. These are manageable with the right security measures in place.

### Conclusion

While OpenID Connect offers additional identity features, OAuth 2.0's flexibility, wide adoption, and focus on authorization make it a strong candidate for a new standard. It provides a robust framework that can be adapted to various use cases, ensuring secure and efficient authorization across different systems and devices. Therefore, I propose that OAuth 2.0 be considered as the primary protocol for new systems, with the option to extend to OpenID Connect if identity features become necessary.

This makes it a more comprehensive solution for both authentication and authorization, aligning well with the decision drivers of security and support for mobile devices. The widespread adoption and future-proof nature of OpenID Connect further strengthen its position as the best choice.", "debate_answer": "OpenID Connect is the best choice for the new standard for authentication and authorization, as it provides a secure, well-reviewed, and comprehensive solution that supports mobile devices and future needs."}

## Context and Problem Statement

As any modern system, Volley Management faces a problem of concurrent changes to data and we need to support such scenario.

We explicitly do not consider an option to go without concurrency checks - time will tell if it is a good decision).

## Decision Drivers <!-- optional -->

* Performance - decision should support high throughput scenarios
* Maintainability - amount of code needed to write should be minimized
human_decision:
# Use Optimistic Concurrency

* Status: ✔ accepted
* Deciders: Sergii Diachenko
* Date: 2021-04-21





## Considered Options

* Optimistic Concurrency
* Pessimistic Concurrency

## Decision Outcome

Chosen option: "Optimistic concurrency", because it is a better from the perfromance perspective and it will support our choice to [use Azure Table Storage](0006-use-azure-table-storage-for-persistence.md).

### Positive Consequences <!-- optional -->

* Concurrency check will fail very rarely so it will have almost no overhead
* It is aligned with Azure Table storage Optimistic concurrency

### Negative Consequences <!-- optional -->

* Whole application will have a responsibility to manage versions properly
* If we change a datastore we might need to have a larger change

## Pros and Cons of the Options <!-- optional -->

### Optimistic concurrency

Storage layer has some indication of 'row version' for each record which changes every time the change to the record is made. When write operation happens it checks for row version match beforehand.

* ✔ Has better performance
* ✔ Azure Table Storage uses Optimistic concurrency model
* ❌ our business logic will have to take this into account

### Pessimistic Concurrency

When something is going to be edited record is locked for change. And everyone waits while operation completes.

* ✔ Easier to implement
* ❌ Terrible performance
* ❌ Is not aligned to Azure Table storage

## Links <!-- optional -->

* Partially a consequence of [ADR-0006](0006-use-azure-table-storage-for-persistence.md)
debate_answer: Versioning is the preferred solution for handling concurrent changes in Volley Management due to its balance of performance, simplicity, and maintainability.
reason: The negative side presents a compelling argument for Versioning as a more suitable solution for Volley Management. It emphasizes the predictability and simplicity of Versioning, which aligns well with the decision drivers of performance and maintainability. Versioning offers a straightforward mechanism for conflict detection and reduces the need for complex retry logic, which can be a drawback of OCC. Additionally, Versioning's flexibility to integrate with other strategies provides a future-proof solution that can adapt to evolving system requirements.
message_history:
To address the problem of concurrent changes to data in Volley Management, we can consider several solutions. Each solution has its own set of pros and cons, and the best choice will depend on the specific requirements and constraints of the system. Here are some potential solutions:

### 1. Optimistic Concurrency Control (OCC)

**Pros:**
- **Performance:** Optimistic concurrency control is generally more performant in scenarios with low contention because it allows multiple transactions to proceed without locking resources.
- **Simplicity:** It is relatively simple to implement, especially in systems where conflicts are rare.
- **Scalability:** It scales well with high throughput scenarios as it minimizes locking.

**Cons:**
- **Conflict Resolution:** Requires a mechanism to detect and resolve conflicts, which can add complexity.
- **Retry Logic:** In case of conflicts, transactions need to be retried, which can lead to increased latency.
- **Data Integrity:** If not implemented carefully, it can lead to data integrity issues.

### 2. Pessimistic Concurrency Control (PCC)

**Pros:**
- **Data Integrity:** Ensures data integrity by locking resources, preventing other transactions from making changes until the lock is released.
- **Conflict Avoidance:** Reduces the need for conflict resolution as it prevents conflicts from occurring in the first place.

**Cons:**
- **Performance:** Can lead to performance bottlenecks, especially in high throughput scenarios, due to locking.
- **Complexity:** More complex to implement and maintain due to the need for lock management.
- **Scalability:** Does not scale well in systems with high contention.

### 3. Versioning (e.g., using timestamps or version numbers)

**Pros:**
- **Performance:** Can be efficient in scenarios where updates are infrequent or can be batched.
- **Simplicity:** Relatively simple to implement and understand.
- **Conflict Detection:** Provides a straightforward way to detect conflicts by comparing version numbers.

**Cons:**
- **Conflict Resolution:** Requires a strategy for resolving conflicts when they are detected.
- **Data Overhead:** May introduce additional data overhead due to storing version information.
- **Complexity in Merging:** Merging changes can become complex if multiple versions need to be reconciled.

### 4. Event Sourcing

**Pros:**
- **Auditability:** Provides a complete history of changes, which can be useful for auditing and debugging.
- **Flexibility:** Allows for easy rollback and replay of events.
- **Scalability:** Can be highly scalable if implemented with a distributed event store.

**Cons:**
- **Complexity:** More complex to implement and understand, especially for developers unfamiliar with the pattern.
- **Performance:** May introduce latency due to the need to process and store events.
- **Data Model Changes:** Requires careful handling of changes to the data model.

### Selecting the Best Solution

Considering the decision drivers of performance and maintainability, **Optimistic Concurrency Control (OCC)** seems to be the best fit for Volley Management. It offers a good balance between performance and simplicity, especially in scenarios with low to moderate contention. While it requires some mechanism for conflict detection and resolution, it avoids the performance bottlenecks associated with locking in Pessimistic Concurrency Control. Additionally, it is easier to implement and maintain compared to more complex solutions like Event Sourcing.

However, the final decision should also consider the specific characteristics of the system, such as the expected level of contention and the importance of auditability and rollback capabilities.
While Optimistic Concurrency Control (OCC) is a viable option, I would argue that **Versioning** might be a more suitable solution for Volley Management, given the decision drivers of performance and maintainability. Here's why:

### Versioning as the Preferred Solution

**Pros:**
- **Performance:** Versioning can be highly efficient, especially in systems where updates are infrequent or can be batched. It allows for quick conflict detection by comparing version numbers, which can be less resource-intensive than other methods.
- **Simplicity:** Implementing versioning is straightforward. It involves adding a version number or timestamp to records, which is easy to manage and understand.
- **Conflict Detection:** Versioning provides a clear and simple mechanism for detecting conflicts. When a transaction attempts to update a record, it checks the version number. If the version has changed since it was last read, a conflict is detected.

**Cons:**
- **Conflict Resolution:** While versioning detects conflicts easily, it still requires a strategy for resolving them. However, this can be managed with well-defined business rules or user intervention.
- **Data Overhead:** There is some additional data overhead due to storing version information, but this is typically minimal and manageable.
- **Complexity in Merging:** Merging changes can be complex if multiple versions need to be reconciled, but this is a common challenge in any concurrency control method.

### Why Versioning Over OCC?

1. **Predictability:** Versioning provides a predictable way to handle concurrency. Each transaction knows exactly what version it is working with, and any changes are based on that version. This predictability can simplify the development and maintenance of the system.

2. **Reduced Retry Overhead:** Unlike OCC, which may require multiple retries in high contention scenarios, versioning can often resolve conflicts more directly, reducing the need for repeated transaction attempts.

3. **Maintainability:** The simplicity of versioning aligns well with the goal of minimizing the amount of code needed. It integrates seamlessly with many existing database systems and frameworks, reducing the need for custom conflict detection logic.

4. **Flexibility:** Versioning can be combined with other strategies, such as OCC or event sourcing, if needed. This flexibility allows for future scalability and adaptability as the system evolves.

### Conclusion

While OCC is a strong contender, Versioning offers a balance of performance, simplicity, and maintainability that makes it particularly well-suited for Volley Management. It provides a straightforward approach to concurrency control that can be easily implemented and maintained, while still supporting high throughput scenarios. However, the final decision should consider the specific characteristics and requirements of the system, including the expected level of contention and the need for auditability or rollback capabilities.

Versioning offers a straightforward mechanism for conflict detection and reduces the need for complex retry logic, which can be a drawback of OCC. Additionally, Versioning's flexibility to integrate with other strategies provides a future-proof solution that can adapt to evolving system requirements.", "debate_answer": "Versioning is the preferred solution for handling concurrent changes in Volley Management due to its balance of performance, simplicity, and maintainability."}

Icons can come in a variety of formats, but SVG is arguably best because it
scales and looks crisp at any size.

Using a library like react-icons makes it easy to import icons from a range
of libraries without having to worry about manually curating and optimizing
files, but we still want to be careful about which licenses we're working
with.

## Decision Drivers <!-- optional -->

* We have a few icons already included, but imported manually
* We don't have any systems currently in place to ensure new icons added
  manually are processed consistently
* There will often be a need to source new icons for different purposes (so
  having a library or two on hand isn't a bad idea)
* The icons (in react-icons specifically) are made available via es-style
  imports which ensures you're only adding weight for the individual icons
  actually used

message_history:
To address the problem of managing and sourcing icons effectively while ensuring compliance with licenses, we can consider the following solutions:

### Solution 1: Use a Centralized Icon Management System

**Pros:**
- **Consistency:** A centralized system ensures that all icons are processed and managed consistently, reducing the risk of errors or inconsistencies.
- **Efficiency:** Streamlines the process of adding new icons, as they can be sourced, licensed, and optimized in one place.
- **Scalability:** Easily scalable as the need for more icons grows, without the need for manual curation.
- **License Management:** Centralized tracking of licenses ensures compliance and reduces legal risks.

**Cons:**
- **Initial Setup:** Requires time and resources to set up the system initially.
- **Maintenance:** Ongoing maintenance is needed to keep the system updated and functioning properly.
- **Complexity:** May introduce complexity, especially if integrating with existing systems.

### Solution 2: Use a Pre-existing Icon Library like React-Icons

**Pros:**
- **Ease of Use:** Libraries like react-icons provide a wide range of icons that are easy to import and use.
- **Optimized Imports:** ES-style imports ensure that only the icons used are added to the bundle, optimizing performance.
- **Variety:** Access to a large variety of icons from different libraries without manual curation.

**Cons:**
- **License Awareness:** Requires careful attention to the licenses of the icons used, as different libraries may have different licensing terms.
- **Limited Customization:** May not offer the level of customization needed for specific design requirements.
- **Dependency:** Relies on an external library, which may introduce dependency management issues.

### Solution 3: Develop a Custom Icon Library

**Pros:**
- **Customization:** Full control over the design and customization of icons to fit specific brand needs.
- **License Control:** Complete control over licensing, as all icons are created in-house.
- **Brand Consistency:** Ensures that all icons align with the brand's visual identity.

**Cons:**
- **Resource Intensive:** Requires significant time and resources to design and develop a custom library.
- **Limited Variety:** May not have the same variety as pre-existing libraries unless a large number of icons are developed.
- **Maintenance:** Ongoing maintenance and updates are needed to keep the library current.

### Best Solution

The best solution depends on the specific needs and resources of the organization. However, a hybrid approach combining Solution 1 and Solution 2 might be optimal:

- **Use a Pre-existing Icon Library (Solution 2):** For general purposes and to quickly access a wide range of icons, using a library like react-icons is efficient and effective.
- **Implement a Centralized Icon Management System (Solution 1):** To ensure consistency, manage licenses, and streamline the process of adding new icons, a centralized system can be implemented alongside the use of a pre-existing library.

This hybrid approach leverages the strengths of both solutions, providing flexibility, efficiency, and compliance with licensing requirements.
While the proposed hybrid approach of using a pre-existing icon library alongside a centralized icon management system is a solid strategy, I would argue that a slightly different approach might be more effective, especially for organizations with limited resources or those prioritizing rapid deployment and flexibility.

### Alternative Best Solution: Prioritize Solution 2 with Enhanced License Management

**Focus on Using a Pre-existing Icon Library (Solution 2):**

1. **Ease of Implementation:** For most organizations, especially those without extensive resources, leveraging a pre-existing library like react-icons offers immediate access to a wide range of icons with minimal setup time. This allows teams to focus on other critical areas of development without getting bogged down in icon management.

2. **Enhanced License Management Practices:**
   - **License Review Process:** Implement a simple, yet effective process for reviewing and documenting the licenses of icons used from the library. This could involve maintaining a shared document or database where licenses are recorded and reviewed periodically.
   - **Training and Awareness:** Educate the team on the importance of license compliance and how to check licenses when adding new icons. This can be part of onboarding or regular training sessions.

3. **Selective Customization:**
   - **Custom Icons for Key Areas:** Instead of developing a full custom library, focus on creating custom icons only for key areas where brand consistency is crucial. This reduces the resource burden while still allowing for brand-specific customization where it matters most.

4. **Regular Library Updates:**
   - **Stay Updated:** Regularly update the icon library to benefit from new icons and improvements, ensuring the library remains relevant and useful.

**Reasons for This Approach:**

- **Resource Efficiency:** This approach minimizes the need for extensive resources and time investment, making it suitable for organizations that need to deploy quickly or have limited budgets.
- **Flexibility and Scalability:** By focusing on a pre-existing library, the organization can easily scale its icon usage without the overhead of managing a custom library or a complex centralized system.
- **Balanced Customization:** Allows for targeted customization without the full burden of developing and maintaining a complete custom library.

In conclusion, while a hybrid approach is beneficial, prioritizing the use of a pre-existing icon library with enhanced license management practices offers a more streamlined and resource-efficient solution for many organizations. This approach balances ease of use, compliance, and the ability to customize where necessary.

## Context and Problem Statement

To deliver BFI's IIIF Universal Viewer auditing platform, custom
deliverables must be produced to support the serving of the Universal
Viewer, and an underlying API which records audit events and persists
them into a database.

This ADR provides options on technologies and frameworks which can be
used to produce these deliverables.

## Decision Drivers

* Ability for Digirati to rapidly deliver the solution.
* Long-term support and stability of the technology and framework
  choices.
* Ease of deployment across different environments / operating systems.
* Robustness and performance of the solution.
* Any technology and framework should be open source.
human_decision:
# Choice of Technology and Framework

* Status: proposed
* Deciders: Daniel Grant <daniel.grant@digirati.com>, Gary Tierney
  <gary.tierney@digirati.com>, Stephen Fraser
  <stephen.fraser@digirati.com>
* Date: 2021-02-16





## Considered Options

* Kotlin (https://kotlinlang.org/) with Spring (https://spring.io/)
* TypeScript (https://www.typescriptlang.org/) with Express.js
  (https://expressjs.com/)
* Python (https://www.python.org/) with Django
  (https://www.djangoproject.com/)

## Decision Outcome

Kotlin is selected as the solution owing to Digirati's vast experience
with the JVM, and when combined with Spring will make this the
deliverables straightforward.

Kotlin does not have the boilerplate cost associated with Java, and as a
result internal there should be fewer barriers in identifying and
understanding the domain specific code required for the deliverables.

In addition, since Spring handles significant amounts of the heavy
lifting, we do not expect to consume time writing code that is not
concerned entirely with handling these audit events.

### Positive Consequences

* Faster development cycle for the parts of the auditing platform which
  require custom code.
* Spring can provide both the Universal Viewer assets and the API,
  unifying everything under one service and keeping authentication
  simple
* The deployment is straightforward, whether using a containerisation or
  deploying directly onto a bare metal server.

### Negative Consequences

* Lack of JVM / Kotlin experience within internal BFI teams.

## Pros and Cons of the Options

### Kotlin with Spring

Spring is a well known framework for building applications on JVM
platforms. It has been the industry leader in the Java space for a long
time and as a result is capable of solving the majority of technical
requirements we'd encounter during the delivery of the solution.

Kotlin is a relatively new technology when compared to Java, however it
compiles to bytecode and thus runs on a JVM. As a result it offers
interoperability both ways between Java and Kotlin code. Kotlin's core
strength is the ability to write code unconstrained by the boilerplate
of Java while still having the entire Java ecosystem available as
tooling.

#### Pros

* 20+ years of combined experience with JVM and Spring applications.
* Tooling for the JVM and Kotlin makes developing the solution a
  plug-and-play experience.
* Spring Boot framework suits itself well to rapid development.
* Zero-configuration OAuth 2.0 / OIDC integration available from Spring.
* JVM as a deployment target may make bare-metal deployment easier when
  running existing JVM applications.
* Rich documentation and examples are available.

#### Cons

* Limited / no experience at BFI with writing code for the JVM.

### TypeScript with Express.js

Express.js is a barebones web server implementation for the NodeJS
platform. The web server itself is mostly a skeleton, which is usually
extended using middleware that is able to intercept requests and
responses, reading and writing to them as needed to add additional
functionality. There is a lot of existing middleware for Express.js
developed by the community that could be used to meet the solution
requirements (e.g. session management, OAuth 2.0 / OIDC integration,
etc).

TypeScript is a loosely typed programming language that sits over the
top of JavaScript and adds strict validation over program source code,
similar to a Java codebase. This allows the writing of code that stays
clear and maintainable over time, by using the type system to document
itself. A fully TypeScript codebase requires that dependencies also have
TypeScript bindings available.

#### Pros

* Digirati have experience building lightweight Express.js applications.
* Rapdi development can be achieved through constant recompilation
  (e.g., watch style commands).

#### Cons

* Less of an "out of the box" solution.
* Poor documentation, outdated examples, frequently changing ecosystem.

### Python with Django

Django is a web framework intended for rapid development in Python.
Similar to Spring, it comes with the majority of the tools that are
needed in building a modern web application out of the box which allows
developers to get started writing domain specific code.

#### Pros

* Internal BFI staff have experience with Python.
* Django is built for rapid development through constant recompilation
  (e.g., watch style commands).
* Rich documentation, lots of examples, stable versioning.

#### Cons

* Digirati have more experience in delivering identity and access
  management with JVM languages.
debate_answer: Solution 2: Python with Django and PostgreSQL is the preferred choice for delivering BFI's IIIF Universal Viewer auditing platform.
reason: The negative side presents a compelling argument for choosing Python with Django and PostgreSQL due to its long-term support, stability, robustness, security, and database consistency. These factors are crucial for an auditing platform that may handle sensitive data and require strong data integrity. While Node.js offers rapid development and high performance, the comprehensive features and reliability of Django and PostgreSQL make them a more suitable choice for this specific use case.
message_history:
To address the problem statement and decision drivers, we can consider several technology and framework options for delivering BFI's IIIF Universal Viewer auditing platform. Here are three potential solutions:

### Solution 1: Node.js with Express and MongoDB

**Pros:**
- **Rapid Development:** Node.js, with its non-blocking I/O and event-driven architecture, allows for rapid development and scalability. Express is a minimal and flexible Node.js web application framework that provides a robust set of features for web and mobile applications.
- **Long-term Support:** Node.js has a large community and is widely used, ensuring long-term support and stability. MongoDB, as a NoSQL database, is also well-supported and scalable.
- **Cross-Platform Deployment:** Node.js applications can be easily deployed across different environments and operating systems.
- **Performance:** Node.js is known for its high performance, especially in handling concurrent requests, which is beneficial for an auditing platform.
- **Open Source:** Both Node.js and MongoDB are open source.

**Cons:**
- **Learning Curve:** If the team is not familiar with JavaScript or Node.js, there might be a learning curve.
- **Database Consistency:** MongoDB, being a NoSQL database, may not be the best choice if strong ACID transactions are required.

### Solution 2: Python with Django and PostgreSQL

**Pros:**
- **Rapid Development:** Django is a high-level Python web framework that encourages rapid development and clean, pragmatic design.
- **Long-term Support:** Python and Django have strong community support and are known for their stability and long-term viability. PostgreSQL is a powerful, open-source object-relational database system with a strong reputation for reliability.
- **Cross-Platform Deployment:** Python applications can be easily deployed across various environments.
- **Robustness and Performance:** Django is known for its robustness and security features, and PostgreSQL offers strong ACID compliance.
- **Open Source:** All components are open source.

**Cons:**
- **Performance:** Python may not be as performant as Node.js in handling a large number of concurrent requests.
- **Complexity:** Django can be more complex to set up and configure compared to lighter frameworks like Flask.

### Solution 3: Java with Spring Boot and MySQL

**Pros:**
- **Long-term Support:** Java is a mature language with a vast ecosystem and long-term support. Spring Boot simplifies the development of new applications and is widely used in enterprise environments.
- **Robustness and Performance:** Java is known for its performance and robustness, and Spring Boot provides a comprehensive framework for building web applications. MySQL is a reliable and widely-used relational database.
- **Cross-Platform Deployment:** Java applications can run on any operating system with a JVM.
- **Open Source:** All components are open source.

**Cons:**
- **Development Speed:** Java applications can take longer to develop compared to those in Node.js or Python.
- **Resource Intensive:** Java applications can be more resource-intensive, requiring more memory and processing power.

### Best Solution Selection

Considering the decision drivers, the best solution would likely be **Solution 1: Node.js with Express and MongoDB**. This solution offers rapid development, strong community support, ease of deployment, and high performance, all of which align well with the decision drivers. Additionally, the open-source nature of the technologies involved ensures long-term viability and flexibility. However, the final choice should also consider the team's expertise and familiarity with the chosen technologies.
While Solution 1: Node.js with Express and MongoDB is a strong contender, I would argue that **Solution 2: Python with Django and PostgreSQL** might be a better fit for the BFI's IIIF Universal Viewer auditing platform, given the context and decision drivers. Here's why:

### Reasons for Choosing Python with Django and PostgreSQL

1. **Long-term Support and Stability:**
   - Python and Django have been around for a long time and have proven their stability and reliability in numerous large-scale projects. The maturity of these technologies ensures that they will continue to receive support and updates, which is crucial for long-term projects.

2. **Robustness and Security:**
   - Django is known for its "batteries-included" approach, offering built-in features for security, authentication, and ORM, which can significantly reduce the risk of security vulnerabilities. This is particularly important for an auditing platform that may handle sensitive data.

3. **Database Consistency:**
   - PostgreSQL is a highly reliable and ACID-compliant database, which is essential for ensuring data integrity in an auditing system. This is a significant advantage over MongoDB, especially if the platform requires complex transactions and data consistency.

4. **Ease of Deployment:**
   - Python applications are known for their ease of deployment across various environments. Django's built-in admin interface and ORM can simplify the management of database records, which can be beneficial for auditing purposes.

5. **Community and Ecosystem:**
   - Python has a vast ecosystem and a supportive community, which can be invaluable for troubleshooting and finding resources. Django's community is also active, providing a wealth of plugins and extensions that can accelerate development.

6. **Open Source:**
   - All components of this solution are open source, aligning with the decision driver for open-source technology.

### Addressing the Cons

- **Performance:**
  - While Python may not match Node.js in handling a large number of concurrent requests, Django's performance is generally sufficient for most web applications. Additionally, performance can be optimized through various means, such as caching and load balancing.

- **Complexity:**
  - Although Django can be more complex to set up initially, its comprehensive documentation and community support can mitigate this issue. The framework's structure can also lead to more maintainable and scalable code in the long run.

### Conclusion

While Node.js with Express and MongoDB offers rapid development and high performance, the robustness, security, and database consistency provided by Python with Django and PostgreSQL make it a more suitable choice for an auditing platform. The decision should ultimately consider the specific requirements of the project and the expertise of the development team, but from a technical standpoint, Solution 2 offers a balanced approach that aligns well with the decision drivers.
{"Whether there is a preference": "Yes", "Supported Side": "Negative", "Reason": "The negative side presents a compelling argument for choosing Python with Django and PostgreSQL due to its long-term support, stability, robustness, security, and database consistency. These factors are crucial for an auditing platform that may handle sensitive data and require strong data integrity. While Node.js offers rapid development and high performance, the comprehensive features and reliability of Django and PostgreSQL make them a more suitable choice for this specific use case.", "debate_answer": "Solution 2: Python with Django and PostgreSQL is the preferred choice for delivering BFI's IIIF Universal Viewer auditing platform."}
comparison_result: 20 - No // The human_decision selected Kotlin with Spring, while the debate_answer preferred Python with Django.

message_history:
To address the problem of HATEOAS links no longer being needed due to the use of OpenAPI for frontend client code generation, we can consider several solutions. Each solution will be evaluated based on the decision drivers: readable API documentation and development effort to test/gather the HATEOAS links.

### Solution 1: Remove HATEOAS Links from the API

**Pros:**
- **Reduced Complexity:** Simplifies the API by removing unnecessary elements, making it easier to maintain and understand.
- **Improved Performance:** Reduces the payload size, potentially improving response times.
- **Lower Development Effort:** Eliminates the need to test and gather HATEOAS links, saving time and resources.

**Cons:**
- **Loss of Hypermedia Benefits:** Removes the ability for clients to dynamically discover actions and resources, which could be useful in some contexts.
- **Backward Compatibility:** Existing clients relying on HATEOAS links may break or require updates.

### Solution 2: Retain HATEOAS Links but Document Them Separately

**Pros:**
- **Backward Compatibility:** Maintains support for clients that still use HATEOAS links.
- **Comprehensive Documentation:** Provides a complete view of the API, including hypermedia capabilities.

**Cons:**
- **Increased Documentation Effort:** Requires additional effort to maintain separate documentation for HATEOAS links.
- **Potential Confusion:** Having separate documentation might confuse developers about the primary method of interaction with the API.

### Solution 3: Deprecate HATEOAS Links Gradually

**Pros:**
- **Smooth Transition:** Allows clients time to adapt to the changes, reducing the risk of breaking existing implementations.
- **Feedback Opportunity:** Provides a period to gather feedback from users about the impact of removing HATEOAS links.

**Cons:**
- **Extended Maintenance:** Requires maintaining both HATEOAS and non-HATEOAS versions of the API during the deprecation period.
- **Delayed Simplification:** The benefits of removing HATEOAS links are not realized immediately.

### Solution 4: Provide a Configuration Option to Enable/Disable HATEOAS Links

**Pros:**
- **Flexibility:** Allows clients to choose whether they want to use HATEOAS links, catering to different needs.
- **Customizable:** Developers can tailor the API response to their specific requirements.

**Cons:**
- **Increased Complexity:** Adds complexity to the API implementation and configuration.
- **Potential for Misconfiguration:** Increases the risk of clients being misconfigured, leading to unexpected behavior.

### Best Solution Selection

Considering the decision drivers of readable API documentation and development effort, **Solution 1: Remove HATEOAS Links from the API** appears to be the best choice. It simplifies the API, reduces development and maintenance efforts, and aligns with the current use of OpenAPI for client code generation. However, it is crucial to ensure that existing clients are informed and supported during the transition to prevent disruptions.
While Solution 1 offers a straightforward approach by removing HATEOAS links entirely, I would argue that **Solution 3: Deprecate HATEOAS Links Gradually** might be a more balanced and strategic choice. Here's why:

### Solution 3: Deprecate HATEOAS Links Gradually

**Pros:**
- **Smooth Transition:** This approach provides a buffer period for clients to adapt to the changes, minimizing the risk of breaking existing implementations. It respects the existing client base and their potential reliance on HATEOAS links.
- **Feedback Opportunity:** By gradually phasing out HATEOAS links, developers can gather valuable feedback from users about the impact of this change. This feedback can inform future API design decisions and ensure that the transition is as seamless as possible.
- **Risk Mitigation:** Gradual deprecation allows for the identification and resolution of unforeseen issues that may arise from the removal of HATEOAS links, reducing the risk of negative impacts on the user experience.

**Cons:**
- **Extended Maintenance:** Maintaining both HATEOAS and non-HATEOAS versions of the API during the deprecation period requires additional resources and effort. However, this can be managed with a clear timeline and strategy for deprecation.
- **Delayed Simplification:** The full benefits of removing HATEOAS links, such as reduced complexity and improved performance, are not realized immediately. However, this delay is a trade-off for ensuring a smooth transition.

### Reasons for Choosing Solution 3

1. **Client Consideration:** A gradual deprecation respects the needs of existing clients who may still rely on HATEOAS links. It provides them with time to adjust their implementations without sudden disruptions.

2. **Risk Management:** By phasing out HATEOAS links, the development team can monitor the impact of the change and address any issues that arise, reducing the risk of negative consequences.

3. **Feedback and Improvement:** The deprecation period allows for the collection of user feedback, which can be invaluable for refining the API and ensuring that it meets the needs of its users.

4. **Strategic Transition:** This approach aligns with best practices for API evolution, where changes are introduced in a controlled and predictable manner, minimizing the risk of breaking changes.

In conclusion, while Solution 1 offers immediate simplification, Solution 3 provides a more considerate and strategic approach to transitioning away from HATEOAS links, balancing the need for simplification with the importance of maintaining a positive user experience.

Python 2 will not be supported by the Python development team past January 1,
2020\. Operating system vendors will provide bugfix support for some number of
years, depending on the OS:

* [Ubuntu 16.04 through 2021](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Releases)

---
layout: page
status: accepted

title: Refactor to Python 3
deciders: "Justin Simpson, Sarah Romkey, Darren Craze, Joel Simpson, David Juhasz, Jesús García Crespo, Pieter Van Garderen, Sara Allain, Ashley Blewer, Santiago Rodríguez Collazo, Miguel Angel Medinilla Luque, José Raddaoui Marín, Cole Maclean, Ross Spencer, Douglas Cerna."
date: 2019-07-22

## Decision drivers

* Archivematica is built on Python 2
* Python 2 will no longer be supported after January 1, 2020
* Archivematica should be refactored to use Python 3 (3.5 at minimum)

## Considered options

1. Refactor to Python 3 (3.5 at minimum)

## Decision outcome

Chosen option: 1, because refactoring Archivematica and all required components
to Python 3 is the only option to ensure that Archivematica continues to be a
safe, stable environment.

Python 3.5 has been set as the minimum version for the following reasons:

* [Python 3.4](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0429/) has already been
  retired
* [Python 3.5](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0478/) is the oldest version
  this is still supported
* Ubuntu 16.04 ships Python 3.5
* Ubuntu 18.04 ships Python 3.6
* For CentOS 7, The Software Collections Repository provides packages for
  Python 3.5 and up

During the course of the project, a higher version of Python may be selected.

[Epic for Python 3 refactoring](https://github.com/archivematica/Issues/issues/805)

### Positive consequences

* Archivematica will be upgraded to a maintained version of Python.
* Archivematica developers will be able to take advantage of new features in
  Python.
* Python 3 support will be maintained for many years (for example, the latest
  stable release is [3.7, which will be maintained until 2023](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0537/#release-schedule);
  further releases are expected).

### Negative consequences

* This is a very large task and components need to be tackled in priority order,
  so it will take a considerable amount of time to get everything refactored.
* Releasing the Python 3 work once all components are refactored will take the
  place of a feature release, leaving some development work unreleased for at
  least 4 extra months (note: all code resides in public branches).
* We will need to support Archivematica running Python 2.7 for some time because
  users will need time to upgrade. This results in a dual support burden.
* Development work currently in progress may need to be refactored to Python 3
  before it are releasable, which is unsponsored work.

## Links

* [PEP 373 -- Python 2.7 Release Schedule](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0373/)
debate_answer: Solution 2: Full Migration to Python 3 is the best approach, as it ensures consistency, long-term viability, and strategic alignment with future technological developments.
reason: The negative side presents a compelling argument for a full migration to Python 3, emphasizing the long-term benefits of consistency, future-proofing, and resource efficiency. While the initial effort is high, the long-term advantages of aligning all components with Python 3 outweigh the temporary benefits of incremental migration and compatibility libraries. The full migration approach addresses the core issue comprehensively and positions Archivematica for future success.
message_history:
To address the problem of Archivematica's lack of compatibility with Python 3, several solutions can be proposed. Each solution has its own set of pros and cons, and the best solution will depend on various factors such as resources, time constraints, and long-term goals.

### Solution 1: Incremental Migration to Python 3

**Pros:**
- **Reduced Risk:** By migrating components one at a time, the risk of introducing bugs is minimized.
- **Manageable Workload:** Developers can focus on one component at a time, making the workload more manageable.
- **Continuous Improvement:** Allows for continuous testing and improvement of each component as it is migrated.

**Cons:**
- **Time-Consuming:** The process can be slow, especially if there are many interdependencies between components.
- **Temporary Incompatibility:** Some components may be on Python 3 while others remain on Python 2, potentially causing compatibility issues.

### Solution 2: Full Migration to Python 3

**Pros:**
- **Consistency:** All components will be on the same Python version, eliminating compatibility issues.
- **Future-Proofing:** Ensures that the entire system is ready for future updates and dependencies that require Python 3.

**Cons:**
- **High Initial Effort:** Requires a significant amount of work upfront to migrate all components at once.
- **Increased Risk:** A full migration increases the risk of introducing bugs across the system.

### Solution 3: Use of Compatibility Libraries

**Pros:**
- **Quick Fix:** Compatibility libraries like `six` can provide a temporary solution to run Python 2 code in a Python 3 environment.
- **Reduced Immediate Workload:** Allows for a gradual transition without immediate full migration.

**Cons:**
- **Temporary Solution:** This is not a long-term solution and will eventually require a full migration.
- **Performance Overhead:** Compatibility libraries can introduce performance overhead and complexity.

### Solution 4: Outsourcing the Migration

**Pros:**
- **Expertise:** Hiring external experts can bring in specialized knowledge and experience in migrating large codebases.
- **Faster Migration:** External teams may be able to complete the migration more quickly than internal teams.

**Cons:**
- **Cost:** Outsourcing can be expensive and may not be feasible for all organizations.
- **Dependency on External Parties:** The organization may become dependent on external parties for future maintenance and updates.

### Best Solution

The best solution would likely be a combination of **Solution 1 (Incremental Migration to Python 3)** and **Solution 3 (Use of Compatibility Libraries)**. This approach allows for a gradual transition with reduced risk and workload, while also providing a temporary fix to keep the system running during the migration process. This combination ensures that the organization can manage the migration effectively without overwhelming resources or introducing significant risks. Additionally, it allows for continuous testing and improvement, ensuring a smoother transition to Python 3.
While the combination of incremental migration and compatibility libraries is a reasonable approach, I would argue that **Solution 2: Full Migration to Python 3** might be the best solution, given the context and long-term goals. Here's why:

### Solution 2: Full Migration to Python 3

**Pros:**
- **Consistency and Simplicity:** Migrating all components at once ensures that the entire system is consistent, eliminating any temporary incompatibility issues that might arise from having some components on Python 2 and others on Python 3.
- **Long-Term Viability:** A full migration future-proofs the system, ensuring that it is ready for future updates and dependencies that require Python 3. This is crucial as more libraries and frameworks drop support for Python 2.
- **Resource Efficiency:** While the initial effort is high, a full migration can be more resource-efficient in the long run, as it avoids the need for ongoing maintenance of compatibility layers and piecemeal updates.

**Cons:**
- **High Initial Effort:** The upfront work is significant, requiring careful planning and execution to ensure a smooth transition.
- **Increased Risk:** There is a risk of introducing bugs, but this can be mitigated with thorough testing and a well-structured migration plan.

### Reasons for Full Migration

1. **End of Life for Python 2:** With Python 2 no longer supported, the urgency to move to Python 3 is high. A full migration addresses this need comprehensively.
   
2. **Dependency Management:** As downstream dependencies drop Python 2 support, maintaining a mixed environment becomes increasingly complex and unsustainable.

3. **Long-Term Cost Savings:** While the initial cost and effort are high, a full migration can lead to long-term savings by reducing the need for ongoing compatibility fixes and updates.

4. **Strategic Alignment:** Aligning all components with Python 3 ensures that the system is strategically positioned for future development and integration with other modern technologies.

5. **Community and Ecosystem Support:** Python 3 has a more active community and ecosystem support, which can be leveraged for future enhancements and troubleshooting.

In conclusion, while the incremental approach with compatibility libraries offers a lower-risk path, the full migration to Python 3 is a more robust and future-proof solution. It addresses the core issue directly and positions Archivematica for long-term success in a rapidly evolving technological landscape.

## Context and Problem Statement

This proposal presents different ways to model associations between resources along with recommendations for when to use them.

## Decision Drivers <!-- optional -->
human_decision:
# 3. Associations and relations between resources

* Status: accepted 
* Deciders: k8s team
* Date: 2019-02-12







### Goals

We want to end up with implementations that meet the following goals:

- Discoverable: it should be easy to understand which resources are associated with each-other. This is important in order to avoid a mess of resources where it's difficult to understand what the final result is going to look like. When associated resources are used by a controller, this fact can be made more apparent by an entry in the status sub-resource.

- Maintenance cost (on-going): a low overhead in terms of coding and keeping resources up to date.

- There should only be one source of truth, usually provided by the user.

- If a resource is user-provided, we should not write to its spec, except in rare circumstances -- and then usually only to a well-defined sub-section. (e.g service controllers writing to `.spec.clusterIP`).

- Have one clear owner of each resource object and avoid having multiple writers unless necessary.

### Prior art

#### Horizontal Pod Autoscaler

The [Horizontal Pod Autoscaler](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/run-application/horizontal-pod-autoscale/) is a controller that scales the number of replicas inside a replication controller / replicaset or deployment based on observed metrics (as of this writing, only CPU is fully supported but others are planned / in progress).

To accomplish this, an `HorizontalPodAutoscaler` resource is created that references a "scale target" (e.g the Deployment to scale), a min/max replica count as well as which metrics to trigger scaling on.

Thus, the configuration is external but to effect the change, the Spec of the target resource is changed.

#### Vertical Pod Autoscaler

The [Vertical Pod Autoscaler](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/verticalpodautoscaler) is similar to the HPA, in that it's configuration is defined externally and it updates a subsection of the `.spec` of the target resources (usually through an abstraction such as the `Deployments` resource to handle rolling out the change).

#### Network policies

The [Network Policies](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/network-policies) in Kubernetes is a specification of how groups of pods are allowed to communicate with each other and other network endpoints.

A `NetworkPolicy` resource is created for each policy, containing a selector for Pods it should be applied to as well as the spec for the policy (ingress/egress rules).

This means the configuration is fully external to the pods. 

#### Pod disruption budget

The [Pod disruption budget](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/pods/disruptions/) feature allows application owners to specify how many pods are allowed to be down simultaneously as a result of voluntary disruptions (e.g routine maintenance, not through unexpected downtime/crashes)

Similar to a network policy, the PDB contains a selector for the pods (for which only a single PDB is semantically allowed to match for each given pod) and a `maxUnavailable` setting.

#### Service

In the [Service](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/service/) resource, `.spec.clusterIP` is written to by the service controller. This is writing into a subsection of a cluster-provided resource instead of being part of the `.status` sub-resource because it's also the source of truth for this IP (it's not stored anywhere else). The user may delete it to have a new one assigned or provide a custom one that should be used. This is a scenario where a user may provide an optional value which the system can generate a default for.

## Considered Options

What follows is an overview of different implementation options with pros/cons. A diagram with the options is provided for convenience.

The example is trying to solve the use case of:

> As an external controller I would like to be able to inject a user into an existing Elasticsearch (for a specific purpose / role). Examples could be a Kibana server user, a remote monitoring user or a user for CCS/CCR operations.

![](resources.png)

In this example, the role definitions are not separate resources, which they /could/ be, but that distinction is not important for the sake of serving as an example.

#### Option 0: Embed/extend instead of introducing a separate resource

Extending the existing resource with some new fields is a way to sidestep the discussion if the use case allows for it.

Pros:
  - Single source of configuration makes it easier to reason about the configuration / state a resource should be in.
  
Cons:
  - If this is a piece of configuration that would be managed by a different controller, it's easy to run into an issue with multiple writers issue where more than one controller or process considers itself the full owner of a resource, thus overwriting the resource from another canonical copy/source.


#### Option 1: Reference through simple label value

Add a simple label key/value pair to the source resources (e.g add `elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/cluster: foo` to the `User` resource in the example).

Pros:
  - Easy to list and watch for from the target controller.
 
Cons:
  - Complex relations could be difficult to model (e.g something that is relevant to more than one cluster would need multiple labels: `source-cluster: ...`, `target-cluster: ...`?) if there's a possibility they exist in the same namespace or could otherwise be overlapping.

    
#### Option 2: Reference through structured label value

Expanding on option 1, we could store some structured data in the label value, allowing to carry.

Pros:
  - Allows expressing more complex relations without additional labels.

Cons:
  - Requires additional client-side filter for list/watch operations.
  - Not easily validated or type-checked by the API, so syntax errors are much more likely to occur.


#### Option 3: Model the association as part of the source spec

Expands on option 2 by defining the association structure as part of the source resource. This is how it's done for `NetworkPolicy` resources.

Pros:
  - Structure of the relation definition is now validated by the API.
  
Cons:
  - Cannot use filtered listing from the API.
  
This option is suitable when the usage direction of the association goes from the source to the target instead of the other way around: e.g something is layered on top of existing functionality and the existing functionality does not need to do anything to integrate.


#### Option 4: One-to-one mapping

If it's a one-to-one mapping between the source and target, the fact that different types can have the same name can be exploited and the association may be on a per-name basis.

![](one-to-one.png)

## Decision Outcome

On a general basis, **option 1** should be preferred, but **option 3** is also considered a good choice if it fits the association order better. **Option 4** is the strongest candidate for one-to-one associations.



### Case study: Kibana-to-Elasticsearch association

Kibana requires:
  - Elasticsearch HTTP(s) URL to know where to connect to.
  - Elasticsearch User to perform maintenance tasks.
  - Secure access to the Elasticsearch cluster CA certificates to ensure encryption.

The Kibana resource and controller mandate that it should be possible to use them without the Kibana instance existing within the same namespace (or within the Kubernetes installation), so these should be possible to provide both manually as well as be managed automatically (for the common case of running both ES and Kibana on Kubernetes).

Thus, we create a new association CRD:

```yaml
apiVersion: k8s.elastic.co/v1alpha1
kind: KibanaElasticsearchAssociation
metadata:
  name: foo-bar
  namespace: elastic
spec:
  elasticsearch:
    name: <name of ElasticsearchCluster resource>
    namespace: foo
  kibana:
    name: <name of Kibana resource>
    namespace: bar
```

The association controller will then ensure that Elasticsearch gets a properly configured Kibana user added:
                           
```yaml
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1alpha1
kind: User
metadata:
  name: foo-bar-kibana
  namespace: foo
  labels:
    # option 1: uses a simple k/v label
    elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/cluster: <cluster id>
spec:
  user: foo-bar-kibana
  password_hash: ...
  <..roles omitted for brevity..>
```

It will also take ownership of the `elasticsearch` section of the Kibana spec:

```yaml
spec:
  elasticsearch:
    url: ...
    auth: ...
    caCertSecret: ... 
```

If the user overwrites this section, it's expected that the association controller will overwrite the users writes.


### Appendix: identified internal use-cases

- Using an ES/Kibana association controller as a more composable alternative to the stack controller:
    - Requires being able to add users to an existing ElasticsearchCluster resource.

- Adding certificates and/or trusted transport node names to clusters for CCR/CCS purposes. The example below is simplified as it does not deal with differences in required configuration between source and target clusters, nor deal with connectivity/discovery information.

```yaml
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1alpha1
kind: CCRConfig
metadata:
  name: foo
  namespace: <cluster-namespace>
  labels:
      elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/cluster: <cluster id>
spec:
  trustedNamesPattern: "*.node.{remote-cluster-id}.cluster.local"
  trustedCA: CA PEM file contents
```

- For remote monitoring, we would like to set up a user that is allowed to ingest to the target cluster. This can be done by adding a new `User` resource in the same namespace as the ingesting cluster with an appropriate option 1 label to be picked up by the ES controller.

- Snapshot repositories is something that we'd potentially want to centrally manage: 

```yaml
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1alpha1
kind: SnapshotRepositoryProvider
metadata:
  name: my-provider
  namespace: elastic
spec:
  credentials:
    secret: <???>
  config: JSON
  clusterSelector:
    matchLabels:
      snapshot-provider: my-provider
```

  Using the above provider we can stamp out instances of the following resource for each matched cluster, and we can potentially keep it up to date with changes to the provider config as well.

```yaml
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1alpha1
kind: SnapshotRepository
metadata:
  name: foo
  namespace: <same as cluster>
  labels:
      elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/cluster: <cluster id>
spec:
  credentials:
    secret: <???>
  config: JSON
```
